syllabus_desc: Explain the environmental lapse rate (ELR) and how NZ conditions typically differ from ISA.
concept: The environmental lapse rate (ELR) is the actual rate at which temperature decreases with altitude in the atmosphere at any given time and place. It's not a constant -- it varies with weather conditions, location, and time of day. The ISA assumes a standard ELR of about 2 degrees C per 1000 ft, but real-world values can range from zero (isothermal) to more than 3 degrees C per 1000 ft (super-adiabatic near hot surfaces). The only way to find the actual ELR is from a radiosonde (weather balloon) or by measuring temperature at different altitudes. NZ conditions typically differ from ISA in predictable ways: summer temperatures are often warmer than ISA at lower levels (especially inland), and the oceanic influence keeps temperatures more moderate than continental locations at similar latitudes. A warm nor'wester across Canterbury can push low-level temperatures well above ISA, affecting aircraft performance. In winter, southerly outbreaks can push temperatures below ISA. Knowing your ISA deviation helps you calculate true altitude and plan aircraft performance accurately.
exam_tip: ELR is the actual measured lapse rate -- it varies. ISA assumes ~2 degrees C/1000 ft. NZ is often warmer than ISA in summer (especially inland) and can be colder in winter southerlies.
key_facts: ELR = actual rate of temperature decrease with altitude (varies with conditions)
ISA assumes ~2 degrees C per 1000 ft -- real ELR can range from 0 to 3+ degrees C
ELR measured by radiosonde or temperature readings at altitude
NZ typically warmer than ISA at low levels in summer
Oceanic influence keeps NZ temperatures more moderate than continental locations
ISA deviation directly affects aircraft performance calculations
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain adiabatic processes, the dry adiabatic lapse rate (DALR), and the saturated adiabatic lapse rate (SALR).
concept: An adiabatic process is a temperature change caused by pressure change, with no heat being added or removed. When air rises, pressure decreases, the air expands, and it cools. When air sinks, it compresses and warms. The DALR (Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate) is 3 degrees C per 1000 ft -- this is the rate at which unsaturated (dry) air cools as it rises. It's a constant because it depends only on physics (the specific heat of dry air). The SALR (Saturated Adiabatic Lapse Rate) is approximately 1.5 degrees C per 1000 ft at lower levels in mid-latitudes -- it's less than the DALR because condensation releases latent heat, partially offsetting the cooling. The SALR isn't truly constant -- it varies with temperature (warmer air holds more moisture, so more latent heat is released, giving a lower SALR). The dew point lapse rate is about 0.6 degrees C per 1000 ft. These three rates are your tools for calculating cloud bases, predicting stability, and understanding the Fohn effect.
exam_tip: DALR = 3 degrees C/1000 ft (constant). SALR = ~1.5 degrees C/1000 ft (varies -- lower in warm air, higher in cold). Dew point lapse rate = ~0.6 degrees C/1000 ft.
key_facts: Adiabatic: temperature change due to pressure change, no heat added/removed
DALR = 3 degrees C per 1000 ft (constant for unsaturated air)
SALR = ~1.5 degrees C per 1000 ft (varies with temperature)
SALR is less than DALR because condensation releases latent heat
Dew point lapse rate = ~0.6 degrees C per 1000 ft
Cloud base height = (surface temp - surface dew point) / 2.4 x 1000 ft
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Determine atmospheric stability using ELR, DALR, and SALR comparisons.
concept: Stability is about whether air, once displaced vertically, wants to keep going or return to where it started. You determine it by comparing the ELR with the DALR and SALR. If the ELR is less than the SALR (say, ELR of 1 degree C/1000 ft), conditions are absolutely stable -- a rising parcel always ends up cooler and heavier than its surroundings, whether it's dry or saturated. If the ELR is greater than the DALR (say, ELR of 3.5 degrees C/1000 ft), conditions are absolutely unstable -- a rising parcel always ends up warmer than its surroundings. The most common and most interesting case is conditional instability: the ELR is between the SALR and DALR (say, 2 degrees C/1000 ft). Here, dry air is stable, but if the air is lifted to its condensation level and becomes saturated, it cools at the slower SALR and may become warmer than the environment -- triggering instability. This is how many NZ thunderstorms develop: conditionally unstable air forced upward by orographic lift or frontal lift until it passes the tipping point.
exam_tip: Stable: ELR < SALR. Unstable: ELR > DALR. Conditionally unstable: SALR < ELR < DALR. Conditional instability is the most common real-world scenario.
key_facts: Absolutely stable: ELR < SALR -- displaced air always returns
Absolutely unstable: ELR > DALR -- displaced air always keeps rising
Conditionally unstable: SALR < ELR < DALR -- stable if dry, unstable if saturated
Conditional instability is the most common real-world situation
Forced lifting (orographic, frontal) can trigger conditional instability
Stability determines cloud type: stable = stratus, unstable = cumulus
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the types, formation, and effects of temperature inversions.
concept: An inversion is a layer where temperature increases with altitude instead of the normal decrease. Inversions are extremely stable -- they suppress vertical motion and act as a lid on weather. There are several types. A radiation inversion forms on clear, calm nights when the ground cools by terrestrial radiation, chilling the air near the surface while air above stays warmer. This is NZ's most common inversion type and the usual cause of morning fog in the Waikato and Canterbury. A subsidence inversion forms when air sinks in a high-pressure system, warming adiabatically as it descends but not reaching the surface -- creating a warm layer aloft that caps the cooler surface air. A frontal inversion occurs at a front where warm air overrides cold air. A turbulence inversion forms at the top of the friction layer where turbulent mixing has created a uniform temperature below, but the air above (unmixed) is warmer. Effects of inversions on aviation: they trap fog, haze, smoke, and pollution below them (reducing visibility), prevent convective cloud from growing through them, and create wind shear at the inversion boundary.
exam_tip: Four inversion types: radiation (clear calm nights), subsidence (high pressure), frontal (warm over cold), turbulence (top of friction layer). All inversions trap moisture and reduce visibility below them.
key_facts: Inversion: temperature increases with altitude (extremely stable)
Radiation inversion: clear calm nights, ground cools by terrestrial radiation
Subsidence inversion: sinking air in high pressure warms aloft, caps surface air
Frontal inversion: warm air overrides cold air at a front
Turbulence inversion: forms at top of friction layer
All inversions trap haze, fog, smoke below -- reducing surface visibility
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Define atmospheric pressure and describe how it is measured.
concept: Atmospheric pressure is the force exerted by the weight of the atmosphere above a given point. At sea level, the ISA standard pressure is 1013.25 hPa -- roughly the weight of a column of air stretching from the surface to the top of the atmosphere. It's measured with a barometer: a mercury barometer balances the weight of the air column against a column of mercury (standard = 29.92 inches or 760 mm Hg), while an aneroid barometer uses a partially evacuated metal capsule that flexes with pressure changes (this is essentially how your altimeter works). Pressure decreases with altitude because there's less atmosphere above you. In NZ aviation, pressure is expressed in hectopascals (hPa), which are numerically identical to millibars (mb). Isobars on a weather map join points of equal MSL pressure, and the spacing between them tells you the pressure gradient -- which directly drives wind speed.
exam_tip: 1 hPa = 1 mb. ISA MSL pressure = 1013.25 hPa = 29.92 inches Hg. Your altimeter is essentially an aneroid barometer calibrated in feet.
key_facts: Atmospheric pressure: force from the weight of the atmosphere above
ISA MSL pressure: 1013.25 hPa (= 29.92 inches Hg = 760 mm Hg)
Measured by mercury barometer or aneroid barometer
Pressure decreases with altitude (less atmosphere above)
NZ aviation uses hectopascals (hPa), numerically identical to millibars
Altimeter is an aneroid barometer calibrated in feet
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AIPNZ GEN section

syllabus_desc: Describe the pressure lapse rate and its variation with altitude.
concept: The pressure lapse rate is the rate at which atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude. Near sea level, it's approximately 1 hPa per 30 ft (or roughly 30 ft per hPa). This is a useful approximation for altimetry corrections. However -- and this is where CPL-level understanding deepens -- the pressure lapse rate isn't constant. It decreases with altitude because air is compressible: the lower atmosphere is denser (compressed by the weight above), so pressure drops faster per unit of altitude gain near the surface. Higher up, the air is thinner and pressure drops more slowly per unit altitude. This means the 1 hPa/30 ft rule works well below about 10,000 ft but becomes increasingly inaccurate at higher altitudes. The pressure lapse rate also varies with temperature: in warm air (less dense), the pressure drops more slowly with altitude than in cold air (denser). This temperature effect is the reason your altimeter over-reads in cold conditions -- the real danger behind the saying 'high to low, look out below' and its temperature equivalent.
exam_tip: Pressure lapse rate: ~1 hPa per 30 ft near sea level. This rate DECREASES with altitude. In cold air, pressure drops faster with altitude -- your altimeter over-reads (true altitude is lower than indicated).
key_facts: Pressure lapse rate: ~1 hPa per 30 ft near sea level
Rate decreases with altitude (air is compressible -- denser at lower levels)
1 hPa/30 ft rule accurate below ~10,000 ft, less so above
Warm air: pressure drops more slowly with altitude
Cold air: pressure drops faster with altitude
Cold air: altimeter over-reads -- true altitude lower than indicated
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AIPNZ GEN section

syllabus_desc: Define QNH, QFE, and standard pressure setting (1013.25 hPa) and explain their application to IFR flight.
concept: These three altimeter settings are the backbone of IFR operations. QNH is the atmospheric pressure at mean sea level, calculated from the station pressure. With QNH set, your altimeter reads altitude AMSL -- this is what you use below the transition altitude for terrain clearance and circuit operations. QFE is the atmospheric pressure at the aerodrome surface. With QFE set, your altimeter reads height above the aerodrome -- reading zero on the ground. QFE is less commonly used in NZ IFR operations but you need to understand it. The standard pressure setting of 1013.25 hPa is used above the transition altitude (in NZ this is 11,000 ft). With standard set, your altimeter reads a pressure altitude called a flight level (e.g., FL 150 = 15,000 ft pressure altitude). Flight levels ensure vertical separation between IFR aircraft because everyone uses the same reference. The transition layer is the airspace between the transition altitude (below which you use QNH) and the transition level (above which you use standard) -- you adjust your subscale when climbing through or descending through this layer.
exam_tip: QNH = altitude AMSL. QFE = height above aerodrome. Standard (1013.25 hPa) = flight levels above transition altitude. NZ transition altitude is 11,000 ft.
key_facts: QNH: MSL pressure -- altimeter reads altitude AMSL
QFE: aerodrome surface pressure -- altimeter reads height above aerodrome (zero on ground)
Standard: 1013.25 hPa -- altimeter reads pressure altitude (flight levels)
NZ transition altitude: 11,000 ft (above this, use standard setting)
Flight levels ensure vertical separation between IFR traffic
Transition layer: airspace between transition altitude and transition level
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AIPNZ ENR section, NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of non-standard pressure and temperature on altimeter readings and the importance of correct subscale setting.
concept: Your altimeter measures pressure and converts it to altitude using ISA assumptions. When conditions differ from ISA, the altimeter lies -- and the errors can be dangerous. Flying from high pressure to low pressure without updating QNH: the altimeter over-reads (you're lower than indicated). The old saying 'high to low, look out below' captures this perfectly. Flying from warm air to cold air: the same problem occurs because cold air is denser, pressure levels are compressed closer to the surface, and your altimeter again over-reads. The temperature error is particularly insidious for IFR flight because it's not corrected by QNH alone -- even with the correct QNH set, your indicated altitude is too high when the temperature is below ISA. In extreme cold (say, an Antarctic outbreak pushing -10 degrees C at sea level over the South Island), the error can be hundreds of feet at higher altitudes. This is why some instrument approach procedures have cold temperature corrections published. Always apply them when the temperature is significantly below ISA. The rule: if it's colder than ISA, you're lower than your altimeter says.
exam_tip: High to low (pressure or temperature), look out below -- altimeter over-reads. Temperature errors are NOT corrected by QNH setting. Apply published cold temperature corrections on instrument approaches.
key_facts: High pressure to low pressure: altimeter over-reads (lower than indicated)
Warm air to cold air: altimeter over-reads (lower than indicated)
'High to low, look out below' applies to both pressure and temperature
QNH corrects for pressure differences but NOT temperature errors
Cold temperature corrections published for some instrument approaches
If colder than ISA: true altitude is lower than indicated altitude
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AIPNZ GEN section, NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Define density altitude and explain its significance to aircraft performance.
concept: Density altitude is the altitude in the ISA at which the current air density would be found. It's a performance metric -- it tells you how your aircraft will perform regardless of what the altimeter reads. Density altitude increases (worse performance) with higher temperature, lower pressure, and higher humidity. On a hot summer day at Christchurch (elevation 123 ft), the density altitude might be 2,000 ft or more -- meaning your aircraft performs as if it's at 2,000 ft in ISA conditions. Takeoff rolls are longer, climb rates are reduced, and engine power output is lower. For CPL operations, density altitude is particularly important for performance planning at higher-elevation NZ aerodromes like Queenstown (1,171 ft), Wanaka (1,142 ft), or any strip in the high country. A combination of high elevation, warm temperature, and low pressure can push density altitude to levels where your aircraft struggles. Always calculate density altitude for takeoff performance planning, especially in summer or at elevated strips.
exam_tip: Density altitude increases with: higher temperature, lower pressure, higher humidity. Always calculate it for performance planning, especially at elevated aerodromes in summer.
key_facts: Density altitude: the ISA altitude at which the current air density would exist
Higher density altitude = worse aircraft performance
Increases with: higher temperature, lower pressure, higher humidity
Affects: takeoff roll, climb rate, engine power output
Critical at elevated NZ aerodromes (Queenstown, Wanaka, high country strips)
Must be calculated for takeoff performance planning
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the forces that generate wind: pressure gradient force, Coriolis force, and friction.
concept: Wind is air moving from high pressure to low pressure -- but it doesn't blow straight from H to L because two other forces get involved. The pressure gradient force (PGF) is the driving force: it pushes air from high to low pressure, and the closer the isobars, the stronger the push. Coriolis force is an apparent force caused by the earth's rotation -- in the Southern Hemisphere, it deflects moving air to the left. Coriolis is zero at the equator and maximum at the poles, and it's proportional to wind speed (faster wind = more deflection). When PGF and Coriolis balance, you get the geostrophic wind -- air flowing parallel to the isobars with low pressure to the right (Southern Hemisphere). But near the surface, friction slows the wind down, which reduces Coriolis deflection, causing the wind to blow across the isobars at an angle toward low pressure. Over land, surface wind is typically about 30 degrees backed from the geostrophic wind and roughly 50-60% of its speed. Over sea, it's about 10-15 degrees backed and 70-80% speed (less friction).
exam_tip: Three forces: PGF (drives wind), Coriolis (deflects left in SH), friction (slows near surface). Geostrophic wind blows parallel to isobars. Surface wind backs ~30 degrees over land, ~10-15 degrees over sea.
key_facts: Pressure gradient force: drives air from high to low pressure
Coriolis force: deflects moving air to the LEFT in the Southern Hemisphere
Coriolis is zero at equator, maximum at poles, proportional to wind speed
Geostrophic wind: PGF and Coriolis balanced, flows parallel to isobars
Friction backs surface wind ~30 degrees and reduces speed to ~50-60% over land
Over sea: ~10-15 degrees backed, ~70-80% of geostrophic speed
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between isobar spacing, pressure gradient, and wind speed.
concept: Closely spaced isobars mean a steep pressure gradient and strong winds. Widely spaced isobars mean a weak gradient and light winds. It's that straightforward on a weather map -- and it's the first thing you should look at when assessing wind conditions. On NZ weather maps, isobars are typically drawn at 4 hPa intervals. If you see four isobars packed into a short distance, you're looking at 16 hPa of pressure change over that distance -- that's a strong gradient and strong winds. The geostrophic wind equation shows this mathematically, but in practice you just read the map: tight isobars = windy. Around curved isobars (lows and highs), the gradient wind differs from the geostrophic wind because centripetal acceleration is involved. Around a low, the gradient wind is less than geostrophic (subgeostrophic), and around a high, it's greater than geostrophic (supergeostrophic). This is a CPL-level distinction that helps explain why weather maps sometimes show stronger winds around highs than you'd expect from the isobar spacing alone.
exam_tip: Tight isobars = strong winds. Around curved isobars: gradient wind is subgeostrophic around lows, supergeostrophic around highs.
key_facts: Closely spaced isobars = steep pressure gradient = strong winds
Widely spaced isobars = weak gradient = light winds
NZ weather maps: isobars typically drawn at 4 hPa intervals
Gradient wind around a low: subgeostrophic (less than geostrophic)
Gradient wind around a high: supergeostrophic (more than geostrophic)
Centripetal acceleration modifies the force balance in curved flow
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the friction layer and the changes in wind velocity when climbing through it.
concept: The friction layer (also called the boundary layer or planetary boundary layer) is the lowest layer of the atmosphere where surface friction influences the wind. Over flat terrain, it extends to about 2,000 ft AGL. Over rough terrain or urban areas, it can extend higher. Over smooth sea, it may be as shallow as 1,000 ft. As you climb through the friction layer, two things happen to the wind: speed increases and direction veers (shifts clockwise in the Southern Hemisphere). At the top of the friction layer, the wind approximates the geostrophic wind -- parallel to the isobars and at full speed. This has direct operational significance: if the surface wind at Christchurch is 270/15, the wind at 2,000 ft AGL might be 300/25 or more. During approach, the reverse happens -- descending into the friction layer, the wind decreases and backs. If you're on approach with a headwind component that suddenly decreases as you descend through the friction layer, you lose airspeed and must compensate. This is a form of low-level wind shear that affects every approach.
exam_tip: Friction layer: ~2,000 ft AGL over land. Climbing through it: wind speed increases and direction veers (clockwise in SH). Descending: wind backs and decreases -- a form of low-level wind shear on approach.
key_facts: Friction layer: surface to ~2,000 ft AGL (varies with terrain)
Over rough terrain/urban areas: deeper. Over smooth sea: shallower
Climbing through friction layer: wind speed increases, direction veers (clockwise in SH)
At top of friction layer: wind approximates geostrophic (parallel to isobars)
Descending through friction layer: wind backs and decreases
This backing and speed decrease is a form of low-level wind shear on approach
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: State Buys Ballot's Law and explain its application in the Southern Hemisphere.
concept: Buys Ballot's Law states: stand with your back to the wind and low pressure is to your RIGHT in the Southern Hemisphere (left in the Northern Hemisphere). It's a practical tool for estimating where pressure systems are relative to your position when you're airborne. At CPL level, you should be able to apply this fluently: if you're flying and the wind is from the west (hitting your tail on an eastbound heading), low pressure is to your right -- that's to the south. If the wind veers from west to northwest during your flight, the low is moving or the pressure pattern is changing. Buys Ballot's Law applies best at the geostrophic level (above the friction layer) because surface friction causes the wind to cross isobars. Near the surface, low pressure isn't directly to your right but slightly ahead and to the right. In practice, this law helps you maintain situational awareness of the pressure pattern during flight, especially if your weather briefing information is ageing or you're outside radio range for updated QNH.
exam_tip: Southern Hemisphere: stand with back to wind, low pressure is to your RIGHT. This works best above the friction layer. Near the surface, low pressure is slightly ahead and to the right.
key_facts: Buys Ballot's Law: back to wind, low pressure to your RIGHT (Southern Hemisphere)
Northern Hemisphere: opposite -- low pressure to your LEFT
Works best above the friction layer (geostrophic wind level)
Near surface: friction modifies it -- low is slightly ahead and to the right
Practical use: estimate position of pressure systems relative to aircraft
Helps maintain weather situational awareness during flight
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Define gusts, squalls, veering, and backing, and describe the diurnal variation of surface wind.
concept: Gusts are rapid, short-lived increases in wind speed -- typically the wind fluctuates above and below its mean speed over periods of seconds. They're caused by turbulent eddies mixing faster air from above the friction layer down to the surface. A squall is a more dramatic event: a sudden increase in wind speed of at least 16 kt, reaching 22 kt or more, lasting at least one minute. Squalls are often associated with the passage of cumulonimbus or cold fronts. Veering is a clockwise wind direction change (e.g., 270 to 300 degrees); backing is anticlockwise (e.g., 270 to 240 degrees). In the Southern Hemisphere, wind veers with frontal passage and with altitude through the friction layer. The diurnal variation of surface wind is important for flight planning: during the day, solar heating creates convection that mixes faster upper-level air down to the surface, increasing surface wind speed and making it gusty. After sunset, convection dies off, the air stratifies, and surface wind decreases and steadies. This is why early morning is often calm (good for fog formation) and afternoon is windiest (good for thermal turbulence).
exam_tip: Gusts = short-lived speed fluctuations. Squall = sudden increase of 16+ kt, reaching 22+ kt, lasting 1+ minute. Veering = clockwise, backing = anticlockwise. Surface wind strongest in afternoon, calmest near dawn.
key_facts: Gusts: rapid short-lived wind speed increases (seconds), caused by turbulent mixing
Squall: sudden increase >= 16 kt, reaching >= 22 kt, lasting >= 1 minute
Veering: clockwise wind direction change. Backing: anticlockwise
Wind veers with frontal passage and with altitude (SH)
Daytime: convection mixes upper air down, surface wind increases and gusts
Night: convection stops, surface wind decreases and steadies
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe sea breezes, katabatic winds, and terrain channelling effects in New Zealand.
concept: Sea breezes develop on sunny days when the land heats faster than the sea: warm air rises over land, cooler air flows in from the sea to replace it. In NZ, sea breezes typically develop by late morning and reach their peak in the afternoon, extending 20-30 km inland with speeds of 10-15 kt (sometimes more). They can dramatically change the wind at coastal aerodromes -- you might depart with a light offshore breeze in the morning and return to a stiff onshore sea breeze in the afternoon. The sea breeze front can trigger convective cloud and showers as it moves inland. Katabatic winds are gravity-driven drainage flows of cold air down slopes and valleys, usually on clear calm nights. They develop when hillside surfaces cool by radiation, chilling the air in contact, which then flows downhill under gravity. In NZ mountain areas, katabatic winds of 10-15 kt are common, and in steep valleys they can be stronger. They contribute to frost and fog formation in valley floors. Terrain channelling occurs when wind is funnelled through valleys, gaps, or passes -- the constriction accelerates the flow. Cook Strait is NZ's classic example: the gap between the North and South Islands funnels the wind, regularly producing speeds 20-30 kt stronger than the surrounding open areas.
exam_tip: Sea breeze: onshore, develops late morning, peak afternoon, 20-30 km inland. Katabatic: downslope at night, clear calm conditions. Terrain channelling: Cook Strait is the classic NZ example.
key_facts: Sea breeze: onshore flow when land heats faster than sea, develops late morning
NZ sea breezes: 10-15 kt, extending 20-30 km inland, peak in afternoon
Sea breeze front can trigger convective cloud and showers inland
Katabatic winds: cold air drainage downslope at night, clear calm conditions
NZ katabatic winds: 10-15 kt common in mountain valleys
Terrain channelling: Cook Strait classic example, wind 20-30 kt stronger than surrounds
source: AFT CPL Meteorology, MetService NZ, AIPNZ MET section

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between temperature, humidity, and dew point.
concept: Warmer air can hold more water vapour than cooler air -- this is the fundamental principle behind most weather phenomena you'll encounter. Relative humidity is the ratio of actual water vapour present to the maximum possible at that temperature, expressed as a percentage. When relative humidity reaches 100%, the air is saturated. Dew point is the temperature to which air must be cooled (at constant pressure) to reach saturation. For aviation, dew point is far more useful than relative humidity because it's a fixed property of the air parcel -- it doesn't change as the temperature changes throughout the day. When the temperature-dew point spread is small (say, 2-3 degrees C or less), expect fog, mist, or low cloud to form soon. If temperature and dew point are converging as the day cools toward evening, you know moisture-related visibility problems are likely. The dew point decreases with altitude at about 0.6 degrees C per 1000 ft, which combined with the DALR of 3 degrees C per 1000 ft gives you the cloud base calculation: (surface temp - surface dew point) / 2.4 x 1000 ft AGL.
exam_tip: Dew point is more useful than relative humidity for aviation. Small temp-dew point spread (2-3 degrees C) = fog/mist/low cloud imminent. Cloud base = (temp - dew point) / 2.4 x 1000 ft AGL.
key_facts: Warmer air holds more water vapour than cooler air
Relative humidity: actual moisture as % of maximum possible at that temperature
Dew point: temperature at which air reaches saturation
Dew point is a fixed property -- doesn't change with temperature fluctuations
Temp-dew point spread < 2-3 degrees C = expect fog/mist/low cloud
Cloud base = (surface temp - surface dew point) / 2.4 x 1000 ft AGL
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the changes of state of water and the role of latent heat in atmospheric processes.
concept: Water exists in three states in the atmosphere: vapour (gas), liquid (water droplets), and solid (ice crystals). Each state change either absorbs or releases energy as latent heat, and this energy transfer drives much of our weather. Evaporation (liquid to vapour) absorbs latent heat -- cooling the surface. Condensation (vapour to liquid) releases latent heat -- warming the air, which is why the SALR is less than the DALR. Freezing (liquid to solid) releases latent heat. Melting (solid to liquid) absorbs latent heat. Sublimation (solid directly to vapour) absorbs a lot of latent heat. Deposition (vapour directly to solid) releases latent heat -- this is how hoar frost forms. At CPL level, the latent heat released during condensation is the energy source that powers thunderstorms and intensifies tropical cyclones. When water vapour condenses inside a growing cumulonimbus, the latent heat release warms the air inside the cloud, making it more buoyant and driving stronger updrafts. This positive feedback is what makes thunderstorms self-sustaining once they develop past the initial cumulus stage.
exam_tip: Condensation releases latent heat (warms air, drives convection). Evaporation absorbs latent heat (cools). Latent heat is the energy source for thunderstorms and tropical cyclones.
key_facts: Evaporation: liquid to vapour -- absorbs latent heat (cooling)
Condensation: vapour to liquid -- releases latent heat (warming)
Freezing: liquid to solid -- releases latent heat
Sublimation: solid to vapour -- absorbs latent heat
Deposition: vapour to solid -- releases latent heat (hoar frost)
Latent heat from condensation powers thunderstorms and tropical cyclones
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the lifting mechanisms that produce cloud and precipitation.
concept: For cloud to form, air needs to cool to its dew point -- and the main way this happens is through lifting. There are four primary lifting mechanisms. Orographic lift: air is forced upward by terrain. NZ's mountainous spine makes this the dominant cloud-forming mechanism -- westerly flow hitting the Southern Alps produces extensive orographic cloud on the western side. Convective lift: the sun heats the surface unevenly, creating thermals of warm buoyant air that rise. This produces cumulus-type cloud, strongest in the afternoon over land. Frontal lift: at a front, warmer air is forced to rise over cooler air. Warm fronts produce widespread stratus and nimbostratus; cold fronts produce narrower bands of heavier convective precipitation. Convergence lift: when air flows together horizontally (into a low or trough), it has nowhere to go but up. These mechanisms often combine -- for example, orographic lift enhancing frontal precipitation on the West Coast of the South Island, where annual rainfall exceeds 6,000 mm in places because every westerly front gets an orographic boost from the Alps.
exam_tip: Four lifting mechanisms: orographic, convective, frontal, convergence. They often combine. NZ's mountains dramatically enhance frontal rainfall on the western side.
key_facts: Orographic lift: air forced upward by terrain -- dominant in NZ due to mountain spine
Convective lift: thermal rising from uneven surface heating -- cumulus cloud
Frontal lift: warm air rises over cold air at a front
Convergence lift: horizontal convergence forces air upward (lows, troughs)
Mechanisms often combine (orographic + frontal on NZ West Coast)
Western South Island: 6,000+ mm annual rainfall from combined orographic and frontal lift
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the types of precipitation and their significance to aviation.
concept: Precipitation types tell you about the atmospheric conditions that produced them, and each type poses different hazards. Rain: water droplets large enough to fall. Continuous rain from nimbostratus (stable lift) indicates warm frontal or occluded frontal activity. Drizzle: very fine droplets from stratus cloud -- associated with stable conditions, often near warm fronts or in the warm sector. The hazard is reduced visibility rather than intensity. Showers: rain from convective (cumuliform) cloud -- typically behind cold fronts or in unstable air masses. They start and stop abruptly and can be heavy. Snow: ice crystals or snowflakes -- forms when cloud temperature is below freezing throughout. In NZ, snow can fall to low levels during strong southerly outbreaks in winter. Sleet: partially melted snow, indicating the freezing level is close to the surface. Hail: ice balls formed in strong thunderstorm updrafts -- large hail can cause serious airframe damage. Virga: precipitation that evaporates before reaching the ground -- it indicates dry air below cloud level and can produce dangerous wind shear beneath the evaporating curtain.
exam_tip: Continuous rain = stable lifting (warm front). Showers = convective cloud (cold front or unstable air). Virga = evaporating precipitation, can produce dangerous wind shear below it.
key_facts: Rain: from nimbostratus (continuous, stable lift) or cumulonimbus (showers, unstable)
Drizzle: fine droplets from stratus, stable conditions, reduces visibility
Showers: convective, start/stop abruptly, can be heavy
Snow: below freezing throughout cloud depth, low-level in NZ winter southerlies
Hail: strong updrafts in thunderstorms, can cause airframe damage
Virga: evaporates before ground, indicates dry air below, associated with wind shear
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the cloud formation process and the conditions required for cloud development.
concept: Cloud forms when air is cooled to its dew point and condensation occurs on condensation nuclei (aerosols). Three things need to come together: sufficient moisture in the air, a mechanism to cool the air to its dew point (almost always through lifting and adiabatic expansion), and the presence of condensation nuclei. In maritime NZ, condensation nuclei are abundant (salt particles from the sea), so cloud formation occurs readily once the other conditions are met. The type of cloud that forms depends on the stability of the air: stable air that's forced to rise produces flat, layered (stratiform) cloud, while unstable air produces heaped, convective (cumuliform) cloud. The height at which cloud forms -- the cloud base -- depends on the temperature-dew point spread at the surface. You can estimate it: cloud base (ft AGL) = (surface temp - surface dew point) / 2.4 x 1000. So if the surface temp is 18 degrees C and the dew point is 12, the spread is 6 degrees, and the expected cloud base is 6/2.4 x 1000 = 2,500 ft AGL.
exam_tip: Cloud needs: moisture, lifting, condensation nuclei. Stable air = stratiform cloud. Unstable air = cumuliform. Cloud base estimate: (temp - dew point) / 2.4 x 1000 ft AGL.
key_facts: Cloud forms when air cools to dew point and condenses on nuclei
Three requirements: moisture, lifting mechanism, condensation nuclei
Maritime NZ: abundant condensation nuclei (sea salt particles)
Stable air + lifting = stratiform (layered) cloud
Unstable air + lifting = cumuliform (heaped) cloud
Cloud base estimate: (surface temp - dew point) / 2.4 x 1000 ft AGL
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: State the altitude limits of high, medium, and low cloud in NZ latitudes, and describe the major cloud types.
concept: Cloud is classified by altitude into three levels at NZ mid-latitudes. Low cloud (surface to about 6,500 ft): stratus (St), stratocumulus (Sc), nimbostratus (Ns), and cumulus (Cu) -- though cumulus can extend much higher. Middle cloud (6,500 to about 20,000 ft): altostratus (As) and altocumulus (Ac) -- identified by the 'alto' prefix. High cloud (above about 20,000 ft): cirrus (Ci), cirrostratus (Cs), and cirrocumulus (Cc) -- composed of ice crystals. Then there are the vertical development clouds that span multiple levels: towering cumulus (TCU) and cumulonimbus (Cb). The naming convention uses building blocks: 'stratus/strato' means layered, 'cumulus/cumulo' means heaped, 'nimbus/nimbo' means rain-bearing, 'alto' means mid-level, and 'cirrus/cirro' means high/ice crystal. At CPL level, you should be able to identify cloud types and immediately infer the atmospheric conditions: cirrus ahead of a warm front tells you the front is 12-24 hours away; towering cumulus tells you the atmosphere is unstable and convective; a low, uniform stratus deck tells you the air is stable with good conditions above the cloud but poor conditions below.
exam_tip: NZ cloud levels: low (surface-6,500 ft), middle (6,500-20,000 ft), high (above 20,000 ft). Know what each cloud type tells you about stability and weather conditions.
key_facts: Low cloud (surface-6,500 ft): St, Sc, Ns, Cu
Middle cloud (6,500-20,000 ft): As, Ac (alto prefix)
High cloud (above 20,000 ft): Ci, Cs, Cc (ice crystals)
Vertical development: TCU, Cb (span multiple levels)
Strato = layered, cumulo = heaped, nimbo = rain, alto = mid-level, cirro = high
Cloud type indicates stability: stratiform = stable, cumuliform = unstable
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, ICAO cloud classification

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and hazards of towering cumulus and cumulonimbus cloud.
concept: Towering cumulus (TCU) and cumulonimbus (Cb) are the most significant cloud types for aviation safety. TCU is a large cumulus with pronounced vertical development but hasn't yet developed the characteristic anvil top. It indicates strong instability and developing convection, with moderate to severe turbulence inside, heavy rain or showers, and the potential to develop into a full Cb. Cumulonimbus is the king of clouds -- it can extend from near the surface to the tropopause (50,000 ft or more in extreme cases). A mature Cb delivers severe turbulence (including inside and near the cloud), heavy rain, hail, lightning, microbursts and wind shear, icing at multiple levels, and in extreme cases, tornadoes. Embedded Cb (hidden within layers of other cloud) is the pilot's nightmare because you can't see it to avoid it. In NZ, Cb development is common along active cold fronts, in the warm sector with moist unstable northerly flow, and over the ranges in summer when convective heating combines with orographic lift. If you're flying IFR and a SIGMET or GRAFOR shows embedded Cb on your route, that's a strong reason to reconsider your flight.
exam_tip: TCU = developing convection, can become Cb. Cb hazards: severe turbulence, hail, lightning, microbursts, icing, wind shear. Embedded Cb is invisible in IMC and extremely dangerous.
key_facts: TCU: large cumulus with vertical development, may develop into Cb
Cb: extends from near surface to tropopause, most hazardous cloud type
Cb hazards: severe turbulence, heavy rain, hail, lightning, microbursts, icing
Embedded Cb: hidden in other cloud layers -- can't see to avoid
NZ Cb common: active cold fronts, moist northerly flow, orographic + convective lift
Embedded Cb on route = strong reason to reconsider flight
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ, AIPNZ MET section

syllabus_desc: Describe the processes that lead to cloud dissipation.
concept: Cloud dissipates when the air warms above the dew point -- causing the water droplets or ice crystals to evaporate back into vapour. This happens through several processes. Subsidence: sinking air warms adiabatically, moving the temperature away from the dew point and dissipating cloud. This is why high pressure systems bring clearing. Advection of drier air: if drier air moves into the region, the dew point drops and existing cloud evaporates. Solar heating: the ground heats up during the day, warming the lower atmosphere and burning off low stratus and fog from below (morning fog 'burning off' is evaporation driven by solar-heated convection). Fohn effect: air descending the lee side of mountains warms at the DALR (if unsaturated), rapidly dissipating any remaining cloud and creating the dramatic Fohn arch and clear skies downwind of NZ's mountain ranges. Rain: cloud droplets falling as precipitation physically remove moisture from the cloud layer. Understanding dissipation helps you forecast when conditions will improve -- for example, predicting when morning low stratus will clear at your destination aerodrome.
exam_tip: Cloud dissipates when temperature rises above dew point. Key mechanisms: subsidence (high pressure), solar heating (morning burn-off), Fohn effect (lee side clearing). Useful for predicting improving conditions.
key_facts: Cloud dissipates when air warms above dew point -- evaporation occurs
Subsidence: sinking air warms, dissipates cloud (high pressure clearing)
Solar heating: surface warming burns off low stratus and fog from below
Advection of dry air: lower dew point causes evaporation
Fohn effect: descending air on lee side warms at DALR, rapid cloud dissipation
Understanding dissipation helps forecast when conditions will improve
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Define prevailing visibility and describe factors affecting visibility for IFR operations.
concept: Prevailing visibility is the greatest visibility reached within at least half the horizon circle, or at least half the aerodrome surface. It's what's reported in METARs. For IFR operations, visibility is one of the two key parameters (along with cloud base) that determine whether you can legally commence or continue an instrument approach. The main factors reducing visibility are precipitation (rain, drizzle, snow), fog and mist (fog is visibility below 1,000 m; mist is between 1,000 and 5,000 m), haze (dry particles -- smoke, dust, pollution), sea spray (coastal areas in strong onshore winds), and blowing snow or dust. At CPL level, you also need to understand slant range visibility -- the visibility you actually experience looking down from an aircraft on approach. Slant range is often less than the reported surface visibility because you're looking through a greater depth of atmosphere at an angle. If the surface visibility is marginal, your slant range visibility may be below approach minima even though the METAR says you're legal. This catches people out.
exam_tip: Prevailing visibility: greatest vis in at least half the horizon circle. Fog < 1,000 m, mist 1,000-5,000 m. Slant range is often LESS than reported surface visibility on approach.
key_facts: Prevailing visibility: greatest vis in at least half the horizon circle
Fog: visibility below 1,000 m. Mist: 1,000-5,000 m
Reduced by: precipitation, fog/mist, haze, sea spray, blowing snow/dust
Slant range visibility: what you see looking down on approach (angle through atmosphere)
Slant range often less than reported surface visibility
Both visibility and cloud base determine IFR approach legality
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AIPNZ MET section

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation and dispersal conditions for radiation fog.
concept: Radiation fog is the most common type in NZ and the one most likely to affect your operations. It forms on clear, calm nights when the ground cools by terrestrial radiation, which chills the air in contact with the surface below its dew point. The conditions needed are: clear skies (to allow maximum radiative cooling), light winds (2-5 kt -- just enough to mix the cooled air through a shallow layer, but not so much that it mixes in warmer air from above), high relative humidity or small temperature-dew point spread, and long nights (autumn and winter, when there's more time for cooling). In NZ, radiation fog is common in sheltered inland valleys -- the Waikato, Canterbury Plains, Manawatu, and the Mackenzie Country are notorious. Katabatic drainage can pool cold air in valley floors, enhancing fog formation. Dispersal occurs when solar heating warms the surface after sunrise, or when wind increases enough to mix the fog layer with drier air above. Radiation fog typically clears from the edges first and may persist in valleys after hilltops clear. Don't assume it will clear quickly -- thick radiation fog in winter can persist into late morning.
exam_tip: Radiation fog: clear skies, light winds (2-5 kt), high humidity, long nights. NZ hotspots: Waikato, Canterbury Plains, Mackenzie Country. Clears after sunrise but can persist into late morning in winter.
key_facts: Forms on clear, calm nights by terrestrial radiative cooling
Needs: clear skies, light winds (2-5 kt), small temp-dew point spread, long nights
NZ hotspots: Waikato, Canterbury Plains, Manawatu, Mackenzie Country
Katabatic drainage pools cold air in valleys, enhancing fog formation
Disperses by solar heating after sunrise or increasing wind
Can persist into late morning in winter -- don't assume quick clearance
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation and dispersal conditions for advection fog and other fog types.
concept: Advection fog forms when warm, moist air moves over a cooler surface, cooling the air below its dew point. Unlike radiation fog, advection fog can form in moderate winds (up to 15 kt) and doesn't need clear skies. In NZ, advection fog is common when warm moist air from the Tasman or subtropics flows over the cooler sea surface south of NZ, then advects onshore. Coastal areas on the east coast of the South Island can experience sea fog when the SST is lower than the air dew point. Advection fog can be persistent and widespread -- it doesn't burn off with solar heating as easily as radiation fog because the fog-forming process (warm air moving over cool surface) continues regardless of the sun. Upslope fog (or hill fog) forms when moist air is forced to rise over sloping terrain, cooling adiabatically to its dew point. On NZ's lower ranges, this simply looks like cloud sitting on the hills -- but if you're flying at those levels, it's fog to you. Frontal fog can form in the rain-cooled air ahead of a warm front when falling rain saturates the air below the cloud base. Steam fog (or sea smoke) forms when cold air passes over much warmer water -- relatively rare in NZ but possible over geothermal areas or warm lakes in winter.
exam_tip: Advection fog: warm moist air over cool surface, can form in moderate wind, doesn't burn off easily. Upslope fog: moist air forced over terrain. Frontal fog: rain-cooled air ahead of warm fronts.
key_facts: Advection fog: warm moist air over cooler surface, can form in winds up to 15 kt
Doesn't need clear skies, doesn't burn off with solar heating as easily
NZ: common when warm Tasman air flows over cooler coastal waters
Upslope fog: moist air rises over terrain, cools adiabatically to dew point
Frontal fog: rain saturates air below cloud base ahead of warm fronts
Steam fog: cold air over warm water (rare in NZ except geothermal areas)
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the operational problems associated with fog and reduced visibility for IFR flight.
concept: Fog and reduced visibility create a cascade of operational problems for IFR pilots. First, approach minima: if visibility (or RVR) is below the minimum for your approach type, you can't legally commence or continue the approach below the decision altitude. This may mean diverting to your alternate -- so your alternate selection and fuel planning must account for fog. Second, even if visibility is technically above minima, marginal conditions make the transition from instruments to visual on approach much more demanding and increase the risk of spatial disorientation. Third, fog often coincides with low cloud bases, compounding the problem. Fourth, fog at your departure aerodrome may prevent takeoff or delay it, cascading through your schedule. For CPL operations, the practical considerations include: always check the TAF trend and temp-dew point spread when planning flights to fog-prone aerodromes, select alternates that are geographically and meteorologically different from your destination, carry sufficient fuel for a missed approach and diversion, and brief the approach thoroughly when conditions are marginal. In NZ, Hamilton (NZHN) and Christchurch (NZCH) are particularly fog-prone.
exam_tip: Fog below approach minima = missed approach and diversion. Always check temp-dew point spread, select meteorologically different alternates, carry diversion fuel. Hamilton and Christchurch are NZ fog hotspots.
key_facts: Fog below minima: can't continue approach below decision altitude -- must divert
Alternate selection must account for fog at destination
Marginal visibility increases workload on the instruments-to-visual transition
Fog often coincides with low cloud bases, compounding the problem
Check TAF trends and temp-dew point spread when planning to fog-prone destinations
NZ fog-prone aerodromes: Hamilton (NZHN), Christchurch (NZCH)
source: AFT CPL Meteorology, AIPNZ MET section, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: List the hazards of airframe icing and describe the conditions under which icing occurs.
concept: Airframe icing occurs when an aircraft flies through visible moisture (cloud or precipitation) at temperatures at or below 0 degrees C. The supercooled water droplets freeze on contact with the aircraft. The hazards are serious and cumulative: increased weight, disrupted airflow over wings and control surfaces (reduced lift, increased drag, potential stall at higher speed), blocked pitot tubes and static ports (unreliable instrument readings), restricted engine air intake, and impaired visibility through windscreen icing. Icing is reported in four intensities: trace (barely perceptible), light (can be managed with de-icing equipment), moderate (short encounters are manageable but prolonged exposure is hazardous), and severe (rate of accumulation is such that de-icing equipment cannot keep up -- immediate exit required). For CPL operations, the critical decision is: can you avoid icing conditions, and if not, does your aircraft have the capability to manage the intensity you'll encounter? Most light singles in NZ have no icing protection beyond carburettor heat -- flying into known icing conditions in these aircraft is asking for trouble.
exam_tip: Icing occurs in visible moisture at or below 0 degrees C. Four intensities: trace, light, moderate, severe. Most light singles have no icing protection -- avoid known icing conditions.
key_facts: Icing occurs in visible moisture (cloud/precipitation) at or below 0 degrees C
Hazards: increased weight, reduced lift, increased drag, instrument blockage
Intensities: trace, light, moderate, severe
Severe icing: de-icing equipment cannot cope -- immediate exit required
Most light singles in NZ have no icing protection
Check freezing level and icing forecasts (GRAFOR, SIGWX) before IFR flight
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology, AIPNZ MET section

syllabus_desc: Describe the types of airframe icing: rime, clear (glaze), and mixed ice.
concept: There are three main types of structural ice, and each forms under different conditions. Rime ice forms when small supercooled water droplets freeze rapidly on contact, trapping air -- it's white, rough, and opaque, like frost in your freezer. Rime ice forms in cloud with small droplets, typically at temperatures between -10 and -20 degrees C. It builds up on leading edges and has a rough profile that disrupts airflow, but it's relatively brittle and easier to remove. Clear (glaze) ice forms when large supercooled droplets hit the aircraft and spread before freezing, creating a smooth, transparent, heavy coating. It forms at temperatures between 0 and -10 degrees C, often in cumuliform cloud or freezing rain. Clear ice is the most dangerous type: it's heavy, difficult to remove, can change the aerodynamic shape of the wing significantly, and can form behind leading edges where de-icing boots don't reach. Mixed ice is a combination of rime and clear, forming when the aircraft encounters a range of droplet sizes and temperatures. In NZ, the worst icing conditions are typically found in active frontal cloud between the freezing level and about -20 degrees C, especially in embedded cumulonimbus.
exam_tip: Rime: small droplets, -10 to -20 degrees C, white and rough. Clear: large droplets, 0 to -10 degrees C, smooth and heavy -- MOST DANGEROUS. Worst NZ icing: frontal cloud between freezing level and -20 degrees C.
key_facts: Rime ice: small droplets, -10 to -20 degrees C, white, rough, brittle
Clear (glaze) ice: large droplets, 0 to -10 degrees C, smooth, heavy, transparent
Clear ice is most dangerous: heavy, hard to remove, alters wing shape
Mixed ice: combination of rime and clear in varying conditions
Worst icing: between freezing level and -20 degrees C in active frontal cloud
Freezing rain (rain falling through sub-zero layer) produces rapid severe clear icing
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Explain the formation and hazards of hoar frost, and describe avoidance and alleviation strategies for all icing types.
concept: Hoar frost forms by deposition -- water vapour freezing directly onto a surface that's below 0 degrees C without going through the liquid phase. On the ground, it forms on aircraft parked overnight when the airframe cools below freezing by radiation and the air temperature is near the dew point. In flight, it can form when descending from very cold air into warmer, moist air. Hoar frost looks like a thin white crystalline coating -- deceptively harmless, but it roughens the wing surface enough to reduce lift and increase the stall speed. Never take off with hoar frost on the wings. The general strategies for dealing with icing: avoidance is always the first option. Check the freezing level and icing forecasts (GRAFOR, SIGWX charts) before flight. If icing is encountered in flight, the primary response is to exit the icing conditions -- climb above the cloud if temperature above is warmer, descend below the freezing level if terrain permits, or divert to an area clear of icing conditions. If your aircraft has de-icing or anti-icing equipment, use it promptly -- don't wait for heavy accumulation. Carburettor icing deserves special mention: it can form at temperatures up to +25 degrees C in humid conditions with low power settings, and it's the most common icing hazard for piston-engine aircraft in NZ.
exam_tip: Hoar frost: deposition on cold surfaces, thin but reduces lift -- never take off with it. Carb ice can form up to +25 degrees C in humid conditions. Icing response: exit conditions (climb, descend, or divert).
key_facts: Hoar frost: deposition (vapour to solid) on surfaces below 0 degrees C
Thin white coating that roughens wing -- never take off with it
In flight: descend below freezing level, climb above cloud, or divert
Use de-icing/anti-icing equipment promptly if available
Carburettor icing: forms up to +25 degrees C in humid conditions
Carb ice most likely: low power settings, high humidity, temp 0 to +25 degrees C
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: State the conditions required for thunderstorm development and describe the three stages of the thunderstorm life cycle.
concept: Three things need to come together for a thunderstorm: sufficient moisture (fuel), instability (the mechanism for sustained vertical development), and a lifting trigger (something to get the air moving upward initially -- orographic lift, frontal lift, convective heating, or convergence). The life cycle has three distinct stages. The cumulus (developing) stage: dominated by updrafts, the cloud grows rapidly upward, no significant precipitation reaches the ground yet. Duration: 10-15 minutes. The mature stage: updrafts and downdrafts coexist -- this is the most hazardous phase. Heavy rain, hail, lightning, severe turbulence, and microbursts occur. The downdraft is driven by precipitation drag and evaporative cooling. Duration: 15-30 minutes. The dissipating stage: the downdraft spreads out, cutting off the updraft's moisture supply. Precipitation weakens, the anvil spreads, and the storm decays. Duration: 30 minutes or more. In NZ, single-cell thunderstorms (the most common type) follow this lifecycle fairly predictably. Multi-cell storms (clusters where new cells keep forming) and the rare supercell can sustain the mature stage for hours.
exam_tip: Three ingredients: moisture, instability, lifting trigger. Three stages: cumulus (updrafts only), mature (most dangerous -- up and downdrafts), dissipating (downdraft dominant, decaying).
key_facts: Three requirements: moisture, instability, lifting trigger
Cumulus stage: updrafts dominant, cloud grows, no significant precipitation (10-15 min)
Mature stage: updrafts AND downdrafts, most hazardous, heavy rain/hail/lightning (15-30 min)
Dissipating stage: downdraft dominant, precipitation weakens, storm decays (30+ min)
NZ: single-cell storms most common, multi-cell and supercell storms possible
Multi-cell storms can sustain the mature stage for hours
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: List the hazards associated with thunderstorms and explain why all aircraft should avoid flight in or near thunderstorms.
concept: Thunderstorms deliver a cocktail of hazards that can overwhelm any aircraft. Severe turbulence: updrafts and downdrafts of 3,000-6,000 fpm can exceed the structural limits of light aircraft and cause loss of control even in larger ones. Hail: large hail can cause major structural damage to airframes, shatter windscreens, and destroy engine components. Lightning: can temporarily blind the crew, damage avionics, and magnetise the compass. Icing: rapid accumulation of clear ice in the updraft region, especially between the freezing level and -20 degrees C. Microbursts: intense downdrafts that spread out on hitting the surface, creating severe wind shear -- a microburst on approach can push an aircraft into the ground. Heavy rain: can cause temporary engine power loss and dramatically reduce visibility. Low-level wind shear: the gust front (outflow boundary) can extend 10-20 nm ahead of the storm. Even if you don't fly through the storm, the area around it is hazardous. The recommendation for light aircraft is to stay at least 20 nm from any observed thunderstorm and never attempt to fly under a Cb -- the downdrafts and wind shear beneath can be lethal.
exam_tip: Stay 20+ nm from thunderstorms in light aircraft. Microbursts create lethal wind shear on approach. Gust front can extend 10-20 nm ahead of the storm. Never fly under a Cb.
key_facts: Severe turbulence: updrafts/downdrafts 3,000-6,000+ fpm possible
Hail: can cause structural damage and shatter windscreens
Lightning: blinds crew, damages avionics, magnetises compass
Icing: rapid clear ice accumulation in updraft region
Microbursts: intense downdrafts causing severe low-level wind shear
Stay 20+ nm from thunderstorms, never fly under a Cb
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology, AIPNZ MET section

syllabus_desc: Describe microbursts and their effect on aircraft operations, particularly on approach and departure.
concept: A microburst is a concentrated, intense downdraft that hits the ground and spreads outward in all directions, creating a starburst pattern of diverging winds. They're typically less than 4 km in diameter and last 5-15 minutes, but in that short time they can create wind shear that exceeds any aircraft's ability to compensate. The classic hazard scenario is on approach: as you fly through a microburst, you first encounter an increasing headwind (performance increases, you may go above glideslope), then the downdraft itself (performance drops, you sink), then a tailwind as you exit the other side (performance drops further, airspeed decreases). The total performance change from headwind to tailwind can be 40-60 kt -- no pilot can react fast enough, especially at low altitude. Wet microbursts are associated with heavy precipitation (the rain drag drives the downdraft); dry microbursts occur when rain evaporates before reaching the ground (virga), cooling the air and creating the downdraft in apparently clear conditions. In NZ, microbursts are associated with vigorous thunderstorm activity along cold fronts and in unstable northwesterly airstreams.
exam_tip: Microburst sequence on approach: headwind increase -> downdraft -> tailwind (performance loss, possible ground impact). Less than 4 km wide, lasts 5-15 minutes. Dry microbursts from virga are particularly insidious.
key_facts: Microburst: intense downdraft hitting ground, diverging outward
Typically less than 4 km diameter, lasting 5-15 minutes
On approach: headwind increase -> downdraft -> tailwind = dangerous performance loss
Total wind shear change can be 40-60 kt
Wet microbursts: driven by precipitation drag
Dry microbursts: virga-driven, can occur in apparently clear conditions
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, ICAO Doc 9817

syllabus_desc: Describe the types of thunderstorms and the conditions that produce them in New Zealand.
concept: NZ experiences several types of thunderstorm activity. Air-mass thunderstorms (or heat thunderstorms) develop on warm summer afternoons when solar heating creates strong convection over land. They're typically single-cell, short-lived, and relatively isolated. In NZ, these are most common over the central North Island plateau and the Canterbury Plains in summer. Frontal thunderstorms develop along active cold fronts where the lifting is forced and widespread. They can produce lines of storms (squall lines) with embedded Cb. These are NZ's most significant thunderstorm hazard because they affect large areas and can produce severe weather. Orographic thunderstorms develop when moist unstable air is forced over mountains. In NZ, the ranges can trigger storms when a moist northwesterly flow interacts with the terrain, especially when the atmosphere is conditionally unstable. Convergence zone thunderstorms can develop where sea breezes converge inland or where wind flow converges along terrain features. The key CPL-level understanding is recognising which type of thunderstorm you're dealing with and how to avoid it -- air-mass storms are often visible and avoidable, but frontal and embedded thunderstorms require weather radar or avoidance of the entire frontal zone.
exam_tip: Air-mass storms: summer afternoons, isolated, avoidable visually. Frontal storms: widespread, embedded Cb possible -- avoid entire frontal zone. Orographic storms: moist unstable flow over ranges.
key_facts: Air-mass (heat) thunderstorms: summer afternoons, single-cell, isolated
Frontal thunderstorms: along active cold fronts, widespread, can form squall lines
Embedded Cb in frontal cloud: most dangerous -- invisible until you're in it
Orographic thunderstorms: moist unstable flow forced over NZ ranges
NZ hotspots: central North Island plateau, Canterbury Plains (air-mass); cold fronts (frontal)
Frontal and embedded storms require radar or avoidance of entire frontal zone
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Define wind shear and describe its effects on aircraft during takeoff, approach, and landing.
concept: Wind shear is any rapid change in wind speed or direction over a short distance, either horizontally or vertically. Low-level wind shear (LLWS) -- below about 2,000 ft AGL -- is the most dangerous because you're close to the ground with limited room to recover. On approach, an increasing headwind that suddenly shifts to a tailwind drops your airspeed and can push you below the glideslope. On takeoff, an initial tailwind component that shifts to headwind as you climb sounds helpful, but the reverse (losing headwind to tailwind) can prevent you from climbing. Sources of LLWS include: thunderstorm outflows and microbursts (the most severe), frontal passages (especially active cold fronts), low-level jet streams, katabatic winds flowing off terrain, and the friction layer transition (backing and decreasing wind on descent). In NZ, LLWS is reported in METARs with a WS group and in pilot reports. When LLWS is reported or suspected at your destination, brief the approach carefully: be prepared for sudden airspeed changes, maintain a slightly higher approach speed if appropriate, and be mentally ready for an immediate go-around.
exam_tip: Wind shear: rapid wind change over short distance. Most dangerous at low level (approach/takeoff). Sources: thunderstorm outflow, frontal passage, katabatic, friction layer. When reported, add speed on approach and be ready for go-around.
key_facts: Wind shear: rapid change in wind speed or direction over short distance
Low-level wind shear (below 2,000 ft AGL) is most dangerous
Sources: thunderstorm outflow, frontal passage, katabatic winds, friction layer
On approach: headwind-to-tailwind shift drops airspeed and sinks aircraft
Microbursts produce the most severe wind shear
When LLWS reported: higher approach speed, prepared for go-around
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology, ICAO Doc 9817

syllabus_desc: Describe convective, mechanical, and clear air turbulence (CAT) including their causes and avoidance.
concept: Convective (thermal) turbulence is caused by uneven surface heating creating updrafts and downdrafts. It's strongest on hot afternoons over land, over dark surfaces (ploughed fields, urban areas), and in unstable air masses. Bumpy below cloud base, smooth above -- it diminishes after sunset. Avoidance: fly early morning or at higher altitudes. Mechanical turbulence occurs when wind flows over rough terrain, buildings, or obstacles, creating eddies downwind. Intensity increases with wind speed and terrain roughness. In NZ, mechanical turbulence can be severe in strong winds near mountain ridges, valley entrances, and in the lee of buildings at airfields. Avoidance: stay well above terrain, avoid lee sides in strong winds. Clear Air Turbulence (CAT) occurs at upper levels, typically near jet streams and tropopause discontinuities. It's invisible -- no cloud, no visual warning. CAT is caused by vertical and horizontal wind shear near the jet stream core, especially on the cold (poleward) side and where the jet stream changes speed or direction rapidly (jet streaks). CAT is reported through PIREPs and forecast on SIGWX charts. Avoidance: use forecasts and PIREPs, and if encountered, change altitude by 1,000-2,000 ft.
exam_tip: Convective: hot afternoons, diminishes after sunset. Mechanical: strong wind over rough terrain. CAT: near jet streams, invisible, forecast on SIGWX. If CAT encountered, change altitude.
key_facts: Convective turbulence: uneven surface heating, strongest afternoon, diminishes after sunset
Mechanical turbulence: wind over terrain/obstacles, increases with wind speed
CAT: near jet streams, invisible, caused by vertical/horizontal wind shear
CAT strongest on cold (poleward) side of jet stream and near jet streaks
CAT reported via PIREPs, forecast on SIGWX charts
If CAT encountered: change altitude by 1,000-2,000 ft
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the Fohn wind effect and its impact on weather on both sides of a mountain range.
concept: The Fohn (or Foehn) effect is one of the most important meteorological phenomena in NZ, given the country's mountainous spine. When moist air is forced up the windward side of a mountain range, it cools at the DALR (3 degrees C per 1000 ft) until it reaches the dew point, then continues to cool at the slower SALR (~1.5 degrees C per 1000 ft) as condensation occurs -- producing cloud and heavy precipitation on the windward side. On the lee side, the now-drier air descends and warms at the DALR all the way down (because the moisture was removed as precipitation). The result: the air arrives on the lee side warmer and drier than it was at the same altitude on the windward side. In NZ, a strong northwest flow produces the classic Fohn effect: heavy rain on the West Coast, while Canterbury and the eastern South Island get hot, dry Fohn winds (the nor'wester). The temperature difference can be dramatic -- 10 degrees C or more. The Fohn arch is the visible cap cloud sitting along the mountain tops with clear skies on both sides. For aviation, the Fohn effect means dramatically different weather on either side of the ranges, plus severe turbulence and mountain wave activity in the lee.
exam_tip: Fohn effect: windward side = cloud and rain (cooling at SALR), lee side = warm and dry (descending at DALR). NZ classic: nor'wester gives West Coast rain, Canterbury warmth. Expect severe turbulence in the lee.
key_facts: Moist air forced up: cools at DALR then SALR, cloud and rain on windward side
After moisture removed, descends lee side: warms at DALR -- arrives warmer and drier
NZ classic: northwest flow = West Coast rain, Canterbury hot dry nor'wester
Temperature difference can be 10+ degrees C between windward and lee
Fohn arch: cap cloud along mountain tops with clear skies on both sides
Lee side: severe turbulence, rotor zones, mountain wave activity
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe mountain wave (lee wave) development, rotor zones, and their dangers to aviation.
concept: Mountain waves (also called lee waves or standing waves) develop when stable air flows over a mountain range. If the wind is strong (at least 20-25 kt perpendicular to the ridge) and the air is stable, the displaced air oscillates vertically downwind, creating a series of standing waves that can extend well above the tropopause and hundreds of kilometres downwind. The key features are: the lenticular cloud (lens-shaped, smooth, stationary -- forms at the crest of each wave where air rises and cools); the rotor zone beneath the first wave crest, which contains extremely turbulent, rotating air (the most dangerous area); and the cap cloud on the mountain itself. Rotor turbulence can be severe to extreme -- aircraft have been destroyed in rotor zones. Mountain waves affect all of NZ's major ranges: the Southern Alps are the most significant, but the Tararua, Kaimai, and Ruahine ranges also produce waves. Visual clues include lenticular clouds, rotor clouds (ragged cumulus beneath lenticular), and the cap cloud. If you see lenticular cloud, expect mountain wave activity and severe turbulence, especially in the rotor zone. Fly at least 50% above the ridge height or avoid the area entirely.
exam_tip: Mountain waves: stable air, strong wind (20-25+ kt) perpendicular to ridge. Lenticular cloud = mountain wave present. Rotor zones = severe to extreme turbulence. Fly 50%+ above ridge height or avoid.
key_facts: Mountain waves: stable air + strong wind (20-25+ kt) perpendicular to ridge
Standing waves can extend above tropopause and hundreds of km downwind
Lenticular clouds: lens-shaped, stationary, mark wave crests
Rotor zones beneath first wave crest: severe to extreme turbulence
NZ: Southern Alps most significant, Tararua/Kaimai/Ruahine ranges also produce waves
Fly at least 50% above ridge height or avoid the area
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ, AIPNZ MET section

syllabus_desc: Describe the meteorological considerations for VFR and IFR flight in mountainous terrain.
concept: Mountain flying in NZ requires respect for terrain-induced weather effects that can change conditions rapidly. For VFR: always cross ridges at least 500 ft above the ridge line, approach at a 45-degree angle (so you can turn away if conditions deteriorate), be aware that cloud can form rapidly on the windward side and may be obscuring passes, and never fly into a valley if you can't see the exit. Downdrafts on the lee side can exceed your aircraft's climb performance -- approach ridges from the windward side where updrafts help. For IFR: published routes through mountain areas have defined minimum altitudes that provide terrain clearance, but in strong wind conditions the turbulence and downdrafts may make maintaining those altitudes extremely difficult. Temperature corrections are critical in cold conditions -- your altimeter over-reads, reducing true terrain clearance. Mountain waves can cause altitude excursions of thousands of feet, making level flight difficult. Always check the GRAFOR and SIGWX for icing, turbulence, and mountain wave forecasts before any flight through or near NZ's ranges. File PIREPs for any significant mountain weather conditions you encounter.
exam_tip: Cross ridges 500+ ft above, approach at 45 degrees from windward side. Lee-side downdrafts can exceed climb performance. IFR: apply temperature corrections in cold conditions, check for mountain wave forecasts.
key_facts: VFR: cross ridges 500+ ft above, approach at 45-degree angle, windward side preferred
Lee-side downdrafts can exceed aircraft climb performance
Never enter a valley VFR if you can't see the exit
IFR: published minimum altitudes assume still air -- wind and turbulence make them harder
Cold temperature corrections critical -- altimeter over-reads, reducing terrain clearance
Mountain waves can cause altitude excursions of thousands of feet
source: AFT CPL Meteorology, AIPNZ ENR section, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe wake turbulence: its causes, factors affecting intensity, and avoidance techniques.
concept: Wake turbulence is caused by wingtip vortices generated by any aircraft producing lift. The vortices rotate inward and downward behind the aircraft, and their strength depends on the generating aircraft's weight, speed, and wing configuration -- heavy, slow, clean (flaps up) aircraft produce the strongest vortices. At CPL level, wake turbulence is a daily consideration because you'll be sharing airspace with aircraft of varying sizes. The vortices sink at about 300-500 fpm and settle about 500-1000 ft below the aircraft's flight path. They drift with the wind -- in a crosswind, the upwind vortex may remain on or near the runway while the downwind vortex drifts away. In calm conditions, both vortices settle onto the runway. Avoidance techniques on departure: rotate before the heavy aircraft's rotation point and climb above its flight path. On approach: stay above the heavy aircraft's glideslope and land beyond its touchdown point. Increase separation time if following a heavy aircraft -- the standard is 2 minutes after a heavy, 3 minutes after a super (A380/An-225 category). Light crosswinds (5-7 kt) are actually the most dangerous because one vortex stays on the runway.
exam_tip: Wake vortices: heavy, slow, clean aircraft produce strongest. Sink 300-500 fpm, settle 500-1000 ft below. Rotate before/land beyond the heavy. Light crosswind (5-7 kt) keeps upwind vortex on runway.
key_facts: Wake turbulence: wingtip vortices from any lift-producing aircraft
Strongest from: heavy, slow, clean (flaps up) aircraft
Vortices sink at 300-500 fpm, settle 500-1000 ft below flight path
Light crosswind (5-7 kt): upwind vortex can remain on runway
Departure: rotate before heavy's rotation point, climb above its path
Landing: stay above glideslope, land beyond heavy's touchdown point
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, ICAO Doc 9426, AIPNZ ENR section

syllabus_desc: Describe how latitude, oceanic surroundings, and topography determine New Zealand's general weather features.
concept: NZ's weather is shaped by three dominant factors. First, latitude: NZ stretches from about 34 to 47 degrees S, placing it squarely in the mid-latitude westerly belt. This means a constant procession of weather systems from the west -- lows, fronts, highs, troughs -- in a repeating cycle. Second, oceanic surroundings: NZ is an island nation surrounded by water with no nearby continental land mass. This maritime influence moderates temperature extremes (warmer winters, cooler summers than continental locations at the same latitude), provides abundant moisture for cloud and rain, and means that air masses reaching NZ have been modified by their ocean passage. Third, topography: NZ's mountainous spine (Southern Alps reaching 3,700 m, plus the North Island ranges) creates a dramatic rain shadow effect. Westerly flow dumps moisture on the western slopes (Fiordland, Westland -- some of the wettest places on Earth), while the eastern regions (Canterbury, Hawke's Bay) sit in the rain shadow and are much drier. The mountain ranges also create mechanical turbulence, mountain waves, and channel wind through gaps like Cook Strait and the Manawatu Gorge.
exam_tip: NZ weather driven by: mid-latitude position (westerly belt), oceanic surroundings (maritime moderation, abundant moisture), topography (rain shadow, turbulence, channelling).
key_facts: Latitude: 34-47 degrees S, mid-latitude westerly belt -- constant W-to-E weather systems
Oceanic: maritime influence moderates temperatures, provides abundant moisture
Topography: mountain spine creates rain shadow (west wet, east dry)
Fiordland/Westland: among wettest places on Earth from orographic enhancement
Canterbury/Hawke's Bay: rain shadow, drier conditions
Mountains create turbulence, waves, and wind channelling (Cook Strait)
source: AFT CPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe westerly and easterly weather situations and their impact on flying conditions across NZ.
concept: Westerly situations occur when the isobars run roughly east-west across NZ with a dominant westerly airflow. This is the typical high westerly index pattern. Western regions get the worst of it: orographic cloud and rain on the ranges, strong winds through gaps and passes, and turbulence. Eastern regions enjoy the rain shadow -- often fine and dry, though nor'westers can be hot and gusty in Canterbury. Westerly situations bring mountain wave activity over the ranges and can produce severe turbulence in lee areas. Easterly situations occur when the pressure pattern drives airflow from the east or northeast onto NZ -- typically when a low sits to the south or southwest and a high to the north or northeast. Eastern regions then cop the weather: onshore cloud, rain, and poor visibility, especially on the east coast ranges (Hawke's Bay, Wairarapa, Canterbury). Northern and eastern NZ can see prolonged periods of low cloud and drizzle in persistent easterlies. The western side of the ranges is now in the rain shadow and may be clear and fine.
