For flight planning, the key is to identify whether you're in a westerly or easterly regime, then anticipate which side of the country will have the good weather and which will have the poor.
exam_tip: Westerly situation: west side wet, east side dry (rain shadow). Easterly situation: east side gets cloud and rain, west side in rain shadow. Identify the regime to plan your route.
key_facts: Westerly situation: W-to-E isobars, western NZ gets cloud/rain, eastern NZ drier
Nor'westers: hot, dry, gusty in Canterbury during westerly situations
Mountain wave and turbulence in lee of ranges during westerlies
Easterly situation: low to south, high to north, east coast gets cloud/rain
Eastern NZ: prolonged low cloud and drizzle in persistent easterlies
Identify the regime (westerly or easterly) to anticipate good and bad weather areas
source: AFT CPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the typical weather associated with southerly and northerly flows across New Zealand.
concept: Southerly flows bring cold polar or sub-Antarctic air over NZ, typically behind a cold front. The characteristics: sharp temperature drop, clearing skies initially followed by convective showers (especially over the sea and coastal areas where the cold air picks up moisture), low snow levels (sometimes to sea level in severe outbreaks), gusty south-to-southwest winds, and good visibility between showers. For aviation, southerlies bring turbulence, shower activity with reduced visibility, and potentially low freezing levels. The southern and eastern coasts of both islands are most exposed. Northerly flows bring warm, moist subtropical or tropical air from the north. Ahead of an approaching front, northerlies increase temperature and humidity, producing extensive cloud, poor visibility in mist or drizzle, and a low freezing level that rises with the warm air. Heavy rain can develop, especially on north-facing ranges. Northerly flows can produce severe thunderstorm activity if the air is sufficiently unstable. For aviation, the main hazards are widespread low cloud, poor visibility, heavy rain, and potentially embedded thunderstorms.
exam_tip: Southerly: cold, showers, low snow, good vis between showers, turbulence. Northerly: warm, moist, extensive cloud, poor vis, heavy rain potential, possible embedded thunderstorms.
key_facts: Southerly: cold polar air, sharp temp drop, convective showers, low snow levels
Southerly: gusty SW winds, good visibility between showers, turbulence
Southern and eastern coasts most exposed to southerlies
Northerly: warm moist subtropical air, extensive cloud, poor visibility
Northerly: heavy rain on north-facing ranges, high freezing level
Northerly: possible embedded thunderstorms in unstable air
source: AFT CPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: For any NZ area, determine which wind directions produce the poorest flying weather.
concept: Every NZ aerodrome has its 'bad weather winds' -- the directions that bring the poorest conditions. The principle is straightforward: wind directions that push moist air onto rising terrain produce cloud and rain. For western aerodromes (Hokitika, Greymouth, Milford): westerly to northwesterly flow is worst (onshore, orographic enhancement). For eastern aerodromes (Christchurch, Napier, Gisborne): easterly to northeasterly flow is worst (onshore, orographic on eastern ranges). For Cook Strait area (Wellington, Paraparaumu): northerly and southerly flows both funnel through the strait creating strong winds, but northerly is typically worst for weather. For Auckland: easterly flow can bring persistent low cloud; northeasterly can bring heavy rain. For Queenstown: northwesterly produces Fohn effects and turbulence; a southerly can bring snow to low levels. At CPL level, you should know the bad-weather wind directions for the aerodromes in your operating area. Check the AIP Vol 4 aerodrome section for meteorological notes, which typically list the problem directions and associated conditions.
exam_tip: Western aerodromes: W-NW worst. Eastern aerodromes: E-NE worst. Wellington: N and S both strong. Queenstown: NW for Fohn/turbulence, S for snow. Check AIP Vol 4 for aerodrome-specific met notes.
key_facts: Western aerodromes (Hokitika, Milford): W-NW winds worst (orographic rain)
Eastern aerodromes (Christchurch, Napier): E-NE winds worst (onshore cloud/rain)
Wellington/Paraparaumu: N and S funnelled through Cook Strait -- strong winds
Auckland: easterly persistent low cloud, NE heavy rain
Queenstown: NW for Fohn/turbulence, southerly for snow
AIP Vol 4 aerodrome sections list specific meteorological hazards
source: AIPNZ Vol 4, MetService NZ, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Using satellite imagery from gopreflight, identify areas of stable and unstable air, frontal cloud bands, and positions of pressure systems.
concept: Satellite imagery on gopreflight is one of your most powerful tools for building the weather picture. On visible imagery (only available in daylight): bright white cloud is thick (deep convective cloud or dense frontal cloud), grey cloud is thinner (stratus), and land and sea are dark. Look for the textured, lumpy appearance of convective (unstable) cloud versus the smooth, uniform appearance of stratiform (stable) cloud. Frontal cloud bands show as organised bands sweeping across the image -- cold fronts often show a sharper, more defined leading edge, while warm fronts have a broader, more diffuse band. On infrared imagery (available day and night): cold cloud tops appear bright white (high cloud = deep convection or cirrus), warm surfaces and low cloud appear darker. The position of the eye or centre of a low can often be identified by the spiral pattern of cloud bands wrapping around it. Highs appear as large clear areas. At CPL level, you should be able to cross-reference the satellite image with the analysis chart to confirm the positions and movement of weather systems. If the satellite shows the front has moved further than the analysis suggests, conditions at your destination may be different from the forecast.
exam_tip: Visible: lumpy = unstable, smooth = stable. IR: bright white = cold/high cloud. Frontal bands sweep across image. Cross-reference satellite with analysis chart to verify system positions.
key_facts: Visible imagery: bright white = thick cloud, grey = thin, lumpy = unstable, smooth = stable
IR imagery: bright white = cold/high cloud, dark = warm surface or low cloud
Frontal bands: organised cloud bands, cold front edge sharper than warm front
Low centres: spiral cloud pattern wrapping around the centre
Highs: large clear areas on satellite
Cross-reference satellite with analysis chart to verify system positions and movement
source: MetService NZ gopreflight, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Using radar imagery from gopreflight, interpret radar returns in terms of precipitation types, intensity, and movement.
concept: Weather radar on gopreflight shows you where precipitation is falling right now. The radar sends out pulses of microwave energy and measures the returns from precipitation -- the stronger the return, the heavier the rain (or the larger the hailstones). Returns are colour-coded: blues and greens indicate light rain, yellows moderate rain, oranges and reds heavy rain. Bright red or magenta returns indicate very heavy rain or hail -- likely thunderstorm activity. At CPL level, you should be able to: identify the movement of precipitation areas by comparing successive images (a loop gives you the speed and direction), distinguish between widespread stratiform rain (uniform, moderate returns over a large area) and convective showers (small, intense cells with strong returns), identify squall lines (narrow bands of strong returns), and estimate when precipitation will reach a specific aerodrome. The limitations of radar are important: it doesn't see cloud without precipitation, the beam can be blocked by terrain (creating radar shadows behind mountains), and at greater distances the beam is higher -- it may miss low-level precipitation. Don't treat radar as the complete picture; use it alongside other met products.
exam_tip: Radar shows precipitation, not cloud. Colour scale: blue/green = light, yellow = moderate, orange/red = heavy, magenta = severe. Watch the loop for movement speed and direction. Radar has terrain shadows and range limitations.
key_facts: Radar shows precipitation returns colour-coded by intensity
Blue/green = light rain, yellow = moderate, orange/red = heavy, magenta = severe/hail
Loop successive images to determine movement speed and direction
Widespread uniform returns = stratiform rain; small intense cells = convective showers
Limitations: doesn't see non-precipitating cloud, terrain shadows, range effects
Use alongside other met products -- not a standalone picture
source: MetService NZ gopreflight, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations of non-aviation-specific weather information.
concept: Not all weather information is created equal for aviation. Public weather forecasts (TV, news apps, general MetService website) are designed for the general public and don't contain the detail pilots need. They use approximate terms ('fine', 'showers'), don't specify cloud bases or visibility in aviation-relevant units, and don't cover hazards like icing, turbulence, or freezing levels. Marine forecasts cover wind and sea state but don't address aviation-specific concerns like cloud base or altimetry. Outdoor recreation forecasts may mention wind and rain but not in the precision you need. At CPL level, you must use aviation-specific products from gopreflight.metservice.com: METARs, TAFs, GRAFORs, SIGMETs, SIGWX charts, and the full suite of aviation forecasts. These are produced to ICAO standards with specific accuracy and verification requirements. General weather information can supplement your aviation briefing -- a public forecast saying 'thunderstorms expected in Canterbury this afternoon' is useful context -- but it can't replace the precision of TAFs and GRAFORs. The risk of relying on non-aviation sources is making decisions based on insufficient or imprecise information.
exam_tip: Public forecasts lack aviation detail (cloud base, visibility, icing, turbulence, freezing level). Always use aviation-specific products from gopreflight. General weather is supplementary only.
key_facts: Public forecasts: approximate terms, no cloud base/visibility/icing/turbulence detail
Marine forecasts: wind and sea state but no aviation-specific information
Aviation-specific products from gopreflight: METARs, TAFs, GRAFORs, SIGMETs, SIGWX
Aviation products produced to ICAO standards with accuracy requirements
General weather information is supplementary context only
Risk of non-aviation sources: decisions based on insufficient/imprecise information
source: AIPNZ GEN/MET sections, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the Inter-Tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ) and its influence on tropical weather.
concept: The ITCZ is the belt of low pressure near the equator where the northeast and southeast trade winds converge. The convergence forces air upward on a massive scale, producing extensive cloud, heavy rainfall, and thunderstorm activity -- it's basically a permanent weather factory. The ITCZ migrates north and south with the seasons, following the thermal equator (the latitude of maximum heating). It reaches furthest south toward NZ during the Southern Hemisphere summer (December-February), when it can extend to about 15-20 degrees S in the South Pacific. The weather in the ITCZ is characterised by: heavy convective rainfall, towering cumulonimbus reaching the tropopause, light and variable surface winds (the 'doldrums'), excellent visibility between showers, and high humidity. For CPL holders who may operate in the Pacific islands region, the ITCZ is a dominant feature. Even for NZ-based operations, the ITCZ's position influences what tropical moisture and weather systems track southward toward NZ.
exam_tip: ITCZ: convergence zone near equator, migrates seasonally. Summer (Dec-Feb) it's furthest south in the South Pacific (~15-20 degrees S). Characterised by heavy convective rain, towering Cb, light variable winds.
key_facts: ITCZ: belt of low pressure where trade winds converge near the equator
Convergence forces air upward -- extensive cloud, heavy rain, thunderstorms
Migrates seasonally: furthest south in Southern Hemisphere summer (Dec-Feb)
In South Pacific: can reach ~15-20 degrees S in summer
Characterised by: heavy convective rain, Cb, light variable surface winds
ITCZ position influences what tropical weather tracks toward NZ
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the development, structure, and lifecycle of tropical cyclones relevant to the South Pacific.
concept: Tropical cyclones develop from tropical disturbances over warm ocean (SST above ~26.5 degrees C) at least 5 degrees from the equator (to get sufficient Coriolis force). The development sequence: tropical disturbance (unorganised convection) -> tropical depression (closed circulation, sustained winds below 34 kt) -> tropical storm (34-63 kt) -> tropical cyclone/hurricane (64 kt or more). The structure is a warm-core system: the eye (10-50 km diameter, calm, clear or broken cloud), the eyewall (most intense winds and rain, towering Cb reaching 50,000+ ft), and the spiral rainbands extending hundreds of kilometres outward. In the Southern Hemisphere, winds spiral clockwise into the centre. The South Pacific cyclone season runs November to April, and cyclones typically form in the Coral Sea, around Fiji, Tonga, and Samoa, occasionally tracking southward toward NZ. As tropical cyclones move south over cooler water, they lose their warm-core structure and transition to extra-tropical systems -- but these ex-tropical cyclones can still deliver destructive winds, torrential rain, and severe flooding when they reach NZ.
exam_tip: Tropical cyclone development needs: SST > 26.5 degrees C, 5+ degrees from equator. South Pacific season: November to April. SH: clockwise winds. Ex-tropical cyclones remain extremely hazardous when reaching NZ.
key_facts: Requires SST above ~26.5 degrees C and at least 5 degrees from equator
Development: disturbance -> depression -> tropical storm (34 kt) -> cyclone (64 kt)
Structure: eye (calm), eyewall (most intense), spiral rainbands
Southern Hemisphere: clockwise wind circulation
South Pacific season: November to April
Ex-tropical cyclones reaching NZ still extremely hazardous
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ, ICAO Annex 3

syllabus_desc: Describe the aviation hazards associated with tropical cyclones and ex-tropical cyclones affecting New Zealand.
concept: Tropical cyclones and their ex-tropical remnants present a suite of aviation hazards. The most obvious are the extreme winds -- sustained hurricane-force winds in the eyewall with gusts far higher, making all flight operations impossible in the core region. Beyond the core: widespread areas of severe turbulence extending hundreds of kilometres from the centre, torrential rainfall causing flooding and reduced visibility, embedded thunderstorms throughout the spiral rainbands, severe icing in the towering convective cloud, and storm surge affecting coastal aerodromes. When an ex-tropical cyclone tracks toward NZ (typically the upper North Island and eastern coasts), it may weaken from its tropical peak but often intensifies as it interacts with mid-latitude weather systems. The result can be extreme rainfall totals (200-400 mm in 24 hours), widespread flooding, damaging winds, and complete closure of affected aerodromes for days. For flight planning, the key is to monitor tropical cyclone advisories and track forecasts from MetService and the Wellington VAAC, plan diversions well in advance, and respect the scale of the event -- an ex-tropical cyclone is not something you plan to 'work around' in a light aircraft.
exam_tip: Tropical cyclone hazards: extreme winds, severe turbulence, heavy rain, embedded Cb, icing, storm surge. Ex-tropical cyclones reaching NZ can dump 200-400 mm rain. Don't try to work around these events -- divert or delay.
key_facts: Eyewall: hurricane-force sustained winds, severe turbulence, extreme rainfall
Spiral rainbands: embedded thunderstorms, severe turbulence, icing
Hazard area extends hundreds of kilometres from centre
Ex-tropical cyclones can intensify interacting with mid-latitude systems
NZ impact: 200-400 mm rainfall in 24 hours, widespread flooding, aerodrome closures
Monitor tropical cyclone advisories from MetService, plan diversions early
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ, Wellington VAAC

syllabus_desc: Describe the systematic process for obtaining and assessing meteorological information for IFR flight planning.
concept: At CPL level, your weather briefing should be systematic, not haphazard. The recommended process is: start broad, then narrow down. First, the big picture: check the analysis chart and prognosis charts to understand the overall weather pattern. Where are the fronts? Where are the highs and lows? What's the general flow pattern? Second, the hazards: check SIGMETs for any active hazards (severe turbulence, icing, volcanic ash, thunderstorms). Check the SIGWX charts for forecast hazards along your route. Third, the route: review the GRAFOR and ROFOR for your planned route. What are the forecast winds at altitude (AAW, wind/temp charts)? What's the freezing level? Where is icing or turbulence forecast? Fourth, the aerodromes: check TAFs for departure, destination, and alternate. Check current METARs for departure conditions. Look at the trend -- are conditions improving or deteriorating? Fifth, the decision: bring it all together. Are conditions above your minima? Can you manage the forecast hazards with your aircraft and equipment? Is your alternate good? Do you have the fuel reserves for a diversion? If any part doesn't stack up, delay or don't go.
exam_tip: Systematic briefing: big picture (analysis/prognosis) -> hazards (SIGMETs/SIGWX) -> route (GRAFOR/ROFOR/AAW) -> aerodromes (TAFs/METARs for dep/dest/alt) -> go/no-go decision.
key_facts: Big picture first: analysis and prognosis charts for overall pattern
Hazards: SIGMETs (active), SIGWX charts (forecast) along route
Route: GRAFOR, ROFOR, AAW, wind/temp charts for en route conditions
Aerodromes: TAFs and METARs for departure, destination, and alternate
Assess trends: compare METAR with TAF -- are conditions tracking forecast?
Decision: minima met? Hazards manageable? Alternate good? Fuel adequate?
source: AIPNZ MET section, AFT CPL Meteorology, NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain the significance of forecast or observed low-level moisture to IFR flight operations.
concept: Low-level moisture is the single best predictor of whether you'll have good or bad flying conditions at your destination. When the temperature-dew point spread is small (3 degrees C or less) at the surface or through the lower levels, expect cloud bases near or at the surface, reduced visibility in mist or fog, and potentially instrument conditions. Watch the trend: if the dew point is steady and the temperature is dropping through the afternoon and evening, the spread is narrowing and conditions will deteriorate. If it's a clear evening with light winds and the spread is closing, radiation fog is likely by dawn. For IFR operations, low-level moisture directly affects: whether you can make a visual approach or need a full instrument approach, whether conditions will be above your minimum descent altitude or decision altitude, whether your alternate remains viable, and whether departure conditions will allow a legal takeoff. The TAF change groups (BECMG, TEMPO) often reflect expected moisture-driven changes -- a TAF showing BECMG BKN005 FG means the forecaster expects the moisture to win and fog to form. Take that seriously. Always check the most recent METAR against the TAF to see which way reality is trending.
exam_tip: Small temp-dew point spread (< 3 degrees C) = low cloud, mist, fog likely. Watch the trend: spread narrowing = conditions deteriorating. Always compare latest METAR against TAF for trend verification.
key_facts: Temp-dew point spread < 3 degrees C: expect low cloud, mist, or fog
Steady dew point + dropping temperature = spread narrowing, conditions worsening
Directly affects: approach type needed, minima, alternate viability, departure legality
TAF BECMG or TEMPO groups often reflect moisture-driven changes
Compare latest METAR against TAF to verify which way conditions are trending
Low-level moisture is the best predictor of good vs poor flying conditions
source: AFT CPL Meteorology, AIPNZ MET section

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate sound meteorological decision-making for IFR flight, including go/no-go and in-flight decisions.
concept: Good met decision-making is the difference between a professional pilot and someone who just holds a licence. The go/no-go decision starts with the big picture: is this a day where the weather is generally workable, or are there serious hazards in play? Then it narrows: for your specific route, at your specific flight time, with your specific aircraft and equipment, are conditions manageable? The answer isn't always binary -- sometimes it's 'go, but with a plan B'. Your plan B might be a different route, a different altitude, a later departure time, or a clearly identified diversion point. In flight, conditions may differ from the forecast. The professional response is to keep updating your picture (new ATIS, new METARs, pilot reports) and be willing to change your plan. Key decision points: if conditions at destination are below your approach minima before you reach your decision point, divert to your alternate rather than trying one more approach. If icing is worse than forecast, descend or divert rather than pressing on. If turbulence is making the flight unsafe, change altitude or divert. The trap that catches pilots is 'get-there-itis' -- the psychological pressure to complete the flight overriding the meteorological reality. At CPL level, you're expected to recognise this trap and resist it.
exam_tip: Go/no-go isn't just binary -- 'go with a plan B' is often the answer. Keep updating your weather picture in flight. Divert early rather than pressing on. Get-there-itis is the trap that kills.
key_facts: Go/no-go assessment: big picture, then route-specific, then aircraft-specific
Plan B: different route, altitude, departure time, or identified diversion point
In flight: continuously update weather picture (ATIS, METARs, PIREPs)
If destination below minima: divert to alternate, don't try 'one more approach'
If icing/turbulence worse than forecast: change altitude or divert
Get-there-itis is the psychological trap -- recognise and resist it
source: AFT CPL Meteorology, NZ CAR Part 91, AIPNZ GEN section

syllabus_desc: Describe the trade winds and the prevailing westerlies in the context of the general circulation.
concept: The trade winds blow from the subtropical highs toward the ITCZ: southeast trades in the Southern Hemisphere, northeast trades in the Northern. They're remarkably steady and reliable -- early sailors depended on them. The prevailing westerlies blow poleward of the subtropical high belt (roughly 30-60 degrees latitude), and NZ sits right in them. The westerlies aren't as steady as the trades -- they're disturbed by the constant passage of lows, fronts, and highs that characterise mid-latitude weather. The strength of the westerlies varies with the westerly index (covered in 20.4.14). Between the trades and the westerlies, around 30 degrees latitude, you find the subtropical high pressure belt -- an area of generally light winds and fine weather (historically called the 'horse latitudes'). The northern tip of NZ occasionally comes under the influence of the subtropical high, especially in summer, bringing settled, warm conditions. Understanding this pattern helps you predict what's coming: if a blocking high moves into the subtropical belt and stops moving, it disrupts the normal westerly flow and NZ's weather pattern changes significantly.
exam_tip: Trade winds: SE trades in SH blow toward the ITCZ. Prevailing westerlies: 30-60 degrees S, dominate NZ's weather. Subtropical highs: ~30 degrees S, light winds, fine weather.
key_facts: Trade winds: SE trades in Southern Hemisphere, blow toward ITCZ
Prevailing westerlies: 30-60 degrees S, NZ sits in this belt
Westerlies are disturbed by passing lows, fronts, and highs
Subtropical high pressure belt: ~30 degrees S, light winds, fine weather
Northern NZ can come under subtropical high influence in summer
Blocking highs in the subtropical belt disrupt normal westerly flow
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of surface type on heating and weather, including land versus sea differences.
concept: The type of surface beneath the atmosphere profoundly affects the weather above it. Land heats and cools quickly (low specific heat, opaque, heat concentrated at the surface). Sea heats and cools slowly (high specific heat, transparent, heat distributed through depth by mixing). This difference drives: the sea breeze/land breeze cycle, the moderation of coastal temperatures compared to inland, and the seasonal lag (sea surface temperature peaks later than air temperature over land). For NZ, surrounded by ocean, this means: coastal areas have mild, moderate temperature ranges; inland areas like the Mackenzie Country and Central Otago have larger daily and seasonal temperature extremes. Dark surfaces (ploughed fields, tarmac, dark rock) absorb more heat and create stronger thermals than light surfaces (snow, sand, light-coloured soil). Wet surfaces heat more slowly than dry surfaces because energy goes into evaporation rather than heating. At CPL level, understanding surface effects helps you predict convective activity, fog formation, and local temperature variations that affect performance calculations.
exam_tip: Land: heats/cools quickly, large temperature range. Sea: heats/cools slowly, small range. Dark surfaces = stronger thermals. Wet surfaces = slower heating (evaporative cooling).
key_facts: Land: low specific heat, heats/cools quickly, large temperature range
Sea: high specific heat, heats/cools slowly, small temperature range
Coastal NZ: moderate temperatures. Inland NZ: larger extremes
Dark surfaces absorb more heat, create stronger thermals
Wet surfaces heat more slowly (energy goes to evaporation)
Surface type affects convection, fog formation, and performance calculations
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between air density and aircraft/engine performance.
concept: Air density directly controls both aerodynamic and engine performance. Wings generate lift by accelerating air over the upper surface -- in thinner (less dense) air, the wing needs a higher true airspeed to generate the same lift, so takeoff and landing speeds increase. Propellers are essentially rotating wings -- they also lose efficiency in thin air. Piston engines rely on air mass flow for combustion -- less dense air means less oxygen per stroke, reducing power output (naturally aspirated engines lose roughly 3% power per 1,000 ft of density altitude). Turbocharged engines maintain sea-level manifold pressure up to their critical altitude, partially compensating. Air density decreases with increasing altitude, increasing temperature, decreasing pressure, and increasing humidity (water vapour is lighter than the nitrogen and oxygen it displaces). These four factors combine into density altitude, which is your single number for performance planning. On a hot day at an elevated NZ aerodrome with low pressure, all four factors may work against you simultaneously -- always calculate density altitude before assuming published takeoff distances and climb rates are accurate.
exam_tip: Air density decreases with: higher altitude, higher temp, lower pressure, higher humidity. Less dense air = longer takeoff, reduced climb, less engine power. Always calculate density altitude.
key_facts: Less dense air: more TAS needed for same lift, longer takeoff and landing
Propeller efficiency decreases in thinner air
Naturally aspirated engines: ~3% power loss per 1,000 ft density altitude
Turbocharged engines maintain power to their critical altitude
Density decreases with: higher altitude, temperature, lower pressure, higher humidity
Calculate density altitude before trusting published performance figures
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe how to determine approximate wind direction from surface observations: ripples on water, wind lanes, smoke, and windsocks.
concept: When you're airborne and need to estimate wind direction without instruments, these visual cues are invaluable. Ripples on water: small ripples align perpendicular to the wind direction, and wind travels from the rougher side toward the smoother side. Wind lanes (dark streaks on water) align with the wind direction. Smoke drifts directly downwind -- a factory chimney or paddock burn gives you a clear wind direction indicator. Windsocks at aerodromes point downwind -- the angle tells you approximate speed (horizontal = 15+ kt, 45 degrees = about 7-8 kt, drooping = light and variable). On the ground, grass and crops lean downwind. For IFR pilots descending below cloud toward an uncontrolled aerodrome without AWIB, these visual clues help you determine the active runway. The limitation of windsocks is that they only show the wind at their exact location and height -- wind can vary significantly across an aerodrome due to terrain, buildings, and thermal effects. In gusty conditions, the windsock swings erratically, giving an indication of gusts but making it hard to determine the mean direction.
exam_tip: Ripples: perpendicular to wind, rougher side is upwind. Wind lanes: parallel to wind. Smoke: drifts downwind. Windsock: horizontal = 15+ kt. All show local conditions only.
key_facts: Ripples on water: perpendicular to wind, rougher side is upwind
Wind lanes (dark streaks on water): align parallel with wind direction
Smoke drifts directly downwind
Windsock: horizontal ~15+ kt, 45 degrees ~7-8 kt, drooping = light/variable
Windsock shows conditions at its location only -- may not represent whole aerodrome
Useful for determining active runway at uncontrolled aerodromes
source: AFT CPL Meteorology, AIPNZ GEN section

syllabus_desc: Explain the significance of the freezing level to IFR flight planning.
concept: The freezing level (or zero-degree isotherm) is the altitude where the air temperature reaches 0 degrees C. It's one of the most important numbers in your IFR weather briefing because it defines the boundary where icing becomes possible. Above the freezing level, any flight in visible moisture (cloud or precipitation) carries an icing risk. The freezing level is shown on GRAFORs, SIGWX charts, and can be calculated from METARs (using the reported temperature and the known lapse rate). In NZ, the freezing level varies dramatically: in a warm northerly flow in summer, it might be 12,000 ft or higher; in a winter southerly outbreak, it can drop to near sea level. When the freezing level is below your planned cruising altitude and you'll be in cloud, you need to consider: does your aircraft have icing protection? Can you safely descend below the freezing level if icing builds? Is there a layer where you can fly above the freezing level but above the cloud? Freezing rain occurs when rain (liquid) falls from a warm layer above into sub-freezing air below -- this produces the most rapid and severe icing and is a reason to avoid flight at all costs.
exam_tip: Freezing level shown on GRAFORs and SIGWX charts. NZ range: near sea level (winter southerly) to 12,000+ ft (summer northerly). Freezing rain = most severe icing -- avoid at all costs.
key_facts: Freezing level: altitude where temperature reaches 0 degrees C
Above freezing level in visible moisture = icing risk
NZ freezing level: near sea level (winter southerly) to 12,000+ ft (summer northerly)
Check on GRAFOR, SIGWX charts; calculate from METAR temp and lapse rate
If flying above freezing level in cloud: need icing protection or escape plan
Freezing rain: most severe icing, occurs when rain falls through sub-zero layer
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AIPNZ MET section, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of stratiform cloud types and their significance for IFR operations.
concept: Stratiform clouds are layered, uniform clouds produced by lifting in stable air. They're your everyday IFR challenge in NZ. Stratus (St): a low, grey, uniform sheet often with a base below 1,000 ft. Produces drizzle, reduces visibility, and is the classic 'instrument conditions' cloud. Forms from cooling of moist stable air (advection, radiation) or from the lifting of fog. Stratocumulus (Sc): a layer of lumpy, rounded masses -- darker than pure stratus, often with some gaps. Bases typically 1,500-6,000 ft. Light turbulence within, drizzle possible. Altostratus (As): a mid-level grey sheet that often thickens ahead of a warm front. The sun may be dimly visible through thin altostratus (a 'watery sun'). Can produce light rain or snow. Nimbostratus (Ns): the rain machine -- a thick, dark, featureless layer producing continuous moderate to heavy rain. Bases often very low (500-2,000 ft) with reduced visibility in rain beneath. This is warm front, occluded front, and sometimes persistent trough weather. For IFR, stratiform cloud means: instrument approaches likely, visibility may be marginal, conditions often persist for hours, and your alternate needs to be somewhere with genuinely better conditions.
exam_tip: Stratiform = stable air, layered cloud. St: low uniform sheet, drizzle. Ns: thick dark layer, continuous rain, very low bases. Stratiform conditions can persist for hours -- plan accordingly.
key_facts: Stratus (St): low grey sheet, bases often < 1,000 ft, drizzle, main IFR cloud
Stratocumulus (Sc): lumpy layer, bases 1,500-6,000 ft, light turbulence
Altostratus (As): mid-level grey sheet, ahead of warm fronts, watery sun visible
Nimbostratus (Ns): thick dark layer, continuous rain, very low bases (500-2,000 ft)
Stratiform conditions persist for hours -- not quick-clearing like showers
IFR in stratiform: instrument approaches, marginal visibility, select different alternate
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Explain how katabatic winds may enhance or inhibit radiation fog formation.
concept: Katabatic winds have a complex relationship with fog formation. Light katabatic drainage (2-4 kt) actually enhances radiation fog formation by gently mixing cooled air through a slightly deeper layer, creating a more uniform fog bank. The drainage also pools cold air in valley floors, where the temperature drops to the dew point across the whole valley. This is why NZ valley locations like the Mackenzie Country, the Waikato lowlands, and inland Canterbury are notorious for morning fog -- cold katabatic air from the surrounding hills drains into the valley overnight and fogs out as a unit. However, if katabatic winds are too strong (above about 5-7 kt), the mixing becomes vigorous enough to bring warmer, drier air from above down to the surface, preventing the temperature from reaching the dew point. So moderate katabatic flow inhibits fog. The sweet spot for fog formation is the light katabatic -- just enough to pool cold air, not enough to mix it away. For flight planning, if your destination is a valley aerodrome and the overnight forecast is for clear skies, light winds, and high humidity, expect fog by dawn.
exam_tip: Light katabatic (2-4 kt): enhances fog by pooling cold air in valleys. Stronger katabatic (5-7+ kt): inhibits fog by mixing in warmer air from above. Valley aerodromes + clear calm night + high humidity = fog by dawn.
key_facts: Light katabatic (2-4 kt): enhances radiation fog by pooling cold air in valleys
Stronger katabatic (5-7+ kt): inhibits fog by mixing warmer air down from above
NZ fog-prone valleys: Mackenzie Country, Waikato lowlands, inland Canterbury
Cold air drainage creates uniform fog banks filling entire valleys
Sweet spot for fog: light katabatic -- enough to pool, not enough to mix
Valley aerodrome + clear night + light winds + high humidity = expect fog
source: AFT CPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe carburettor icing: conditions, temperature ranges, and the effect of throttle setting.
concept: Carburettor icing is the most common icing hazard for piston-engine aircraft in NZ, and it can occur in conditions you'd never associate with 'icing weather'. It forms because of the temperature drop in the carburettor caused by two processes: fuel vaporisation (absorbs latent heat, cooling the air by about 15 degrees C) and the venturi effect (air accelerating through the carburettor throat drops in pressure and temperature). This combined cooling can freeze the moisture in the air, forming ice in the carburettor throat and restricting airflow to the engine. The dangerous temperature range for carburettor ice is roughly -5 to +25 degrees C with visible moisture or high humidity -- far warmer than you'd expect for 'icing'. The most dangerous combination is temperatures between +5 and +15 degrees C with high humidity. Throttle setting matters: at low power (descent, taxiing), the throttle plate creates more restriction and turbulence, enhancing the cooling effect and making icing more likely. At full power, the throttle is wide open with less restriction, and the engine generates more heat. This is why carb ice is most dangerous during descent and idle power operations. Prevention: apply carburettor heat before reducing power, and use it regularly during cruise in icing-prone conditions.
exam_tip: Carb ice: -5 to +25 degrees C, worst at +5 to +15 with high humidity. Low power settings increase risk. Apply carb heat BEFORE reducing power. Most common icing hazard for NZ piston aircraft.
key_facts: Carb ice from fuel vaporisation (~15 degrees C cooling) and venturi effect
Dangerous range: -5 to +25 degrees C with moisture/high humidity
Most dangerous: +5 to +15 degrees C with high humidity
Low power settings increase risk (throttle restriction enhances cooling)
Apply carburettor heat before reducing power
Symptoms: gradual RPM drop (fixed pitch) or manifold pressure drop (constant speed)
source: AFT CPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Aerodynamics and Systems

syllabus_desc: Describe the gust front and squall line phenomena associated with thunderstorms.
concept: The gust front is the leading edge of the cold air outflow from a thunderstorm's downdraft. As the downdraft hits the ground and spreads outward, it pushes ahead of the storm as a wedge of cold air -- like a mini cold front. The gust front can extend 10-20 nm ahead of the visible storm, and it brings a sudden wind shift, a sharp drop in temperature, a spike in pressure (the pressure jump), and gusty winds. For aircraft on approach, hitting an unexpected gust front means sudden wind shear and turbulence with no visual warning of the storm's proximity. A squall line is a line of thunderstorms, often along or ahead of a cold front, where the gust fronts from individual storms merge into a continuous line. Squall lines can be hundreds of kilometres long and present a wall of severe weather that's extremely difficult to penetrate safely. In NZ, squall lines typically develop along active cold fronts crossing from the Tasman, especially when the front is moving quickly and the pre-frontal air is moist and unstable. If a squall line is between you and your destination, don't try to find a gap -- the gaps close quickly. Divert around the end of the line or wait for it to pass.
exam_tip: Gust front: outflow boundary 10-20 nm ahead of storm, brings sudden wind shift and turbulence. Squall line: continuous line of Cb, often along cold fronts. Don't try to find gaps in a squall line -- divert or wait.
key_facts: Gust front: leading edge of thunderstorm outflow, extends 10-20 nm ahead
Brings: sudden wind shift, temperature drop, pressure jump, gusty winds
Can cause unexpected wind shear with no visible storm nearby
Squall line: merged gust fronts forming continuous line of thunderstorms
NZ squall lines: along active cold fronts from the Tasman
Never try to penetrate a squall line -- divert around or wait
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the ICAO turbulence intensity scale and its operational significance.
concept: Turbulence is reported using the ICAO intensity scale: light, moderate, severe, and extreme. Light turbulence causes slight, erratic changes in altitude and attitude -- occupants feel a slight strain against seat belts, and unsecured objects shift slightly. It's common and generally manageable. Moderate turbulence causes definite changes in altitude and attitude but the aircraft remains in positive control -- occupants feel definite strain against belts, and unsecured objects are dislodged. It's uncomfortable and increases pilot workload. Severe turbulence causes large, abrupt changes in altitude and attitude, and the aircraft may momentarily be out of control -- occupants are forced violently against belts, and unsecured objects are thrown. Severe turbulence can cause structural damage and injury. Extreme turbulence is practically uncontrollable -- the aircraft is violently tossed about and structural damage is possible. When filing PIREPs, use the correct terminology so other pilots and ATC can assess the hazard accurately. At CPL level, encountering moderate turbulence should prompt you to slow to the aircraft's turbulence penetration speed (Va) and consider changing altitude or route. Severe turbulence demands immediate action: Va speed, maintain attitude, and exit the area.
exam_tip: Turbulence scale: light (slight changes), moderate (definite changes, aircraft controlled), severe (large abrupt changes, momentary loss of control), extreme (practically uncontrollable). Moderate or worse: slow to Va.
key_facts: Light: slight erratic changes, slight strain on belts
Moderate: definite altitude/attitude changes, aircraft in positive control
Severe: large abrupt changes, momentary loss of control possible
Extreme: practically uncontrollable, structural damage possible
Moderate or worse: slow to turbulence penetration speed (Va)
Use correct ICAO terminology in PIREPs for other pilots to assess the hazard
source: ICAO Annex 3, AFT ATPL Meteorology, AIPNZ MET section

syllabus_desc: Describe the seasonal variation of New Zealand weather and its impact on aviation operations.
concept: NZ weather has distinct seasonal patterns that affect flight planning. Summer (December-February): longer days mean less radiation fog (shorter nights), but stronger convective activity in the afternoon. The subtropical high tends to be further south, bringing more settled periods. Thunderstorms are more common, especially over inland areas. The freezing level is higher (often above 10,000 ft), reducing icing risk at lower altitudes. Tropical cyclone remnants can affect the North Island. Autumn (March-May): radiation fog becomes increasingly common as nights lengthen. Frontal activity picks up. Sea surface temperatures are still relatively warm, providing moisture for weather systems. Winter (June-August): shortest days, longest nights. Radiation fog peaks. Freezing levels can drop near sea level in southerly outbreaks, with snow to low levels. Mountain wave activity is common with strong westerly flow. Fewer thunderstorms but vigorous frontal systems. Spring (September-November): weather is variable -- rapidly alternating fine and poor days. Spring is often NZ's windiest season. Convective activity increases as the sun strengthens but the atmosphere is still cool aloft (good instability). For CPL operations, each season demands different weather awareness priorities.
exam_tip: Summer: convection, high freezing level, possible tropical cyclones. Winter: fog, low freezing level, mountain waves. Spring: windiest, variable. Autumn: increasing fog, active fronts.
key_facts: Summer: convective activity, higher freezing level, possible tropical cyclone remnants
Autumn: increasing radiation fog, active fronts, warm SST provides moisture
Winter: peak fog, low freezing levels, snow to low levels, mountain waves
Spring: windiest season, variable weather, increasing convection
Each season demands different weather awareness priorities
Seasonal freezing level range: near sea level (winter) to 10,000+ ft (summer)
source: MetService NZ, AFT CPL Meteorology

topic: 20.2 -- Regional Meteorological Services, Reports and Forecasts
syllabus_desc: Explain in plain language the coded or pictorial information contained in a TREND forecast.
concept: A TREND forecast is a short-period forecast appended to a METAR or SPECI, covering the next 2 hours from the time of observation. It uses change indicators BECMG (becoming -- a permanent change expected) or TEMPO (temporary fluctuations) to describe expected changes in wind, visibility, weather, and cloud. If no significant change is expected, the TREND group reads NOSIG (no significant change). At CPL level, the TREND is a quick-look tool for assessing whether conditions at your destination are likely to improve or deteriorate before you arrive. If you're planning an approach and the METAR shows marginal conditions with a TREND showing BECMG BKN003 FG, that's telling you conditions are expected to drop below most approach minima within 2 hours. Conversely, BECMG FEW030 tells you conditions are expected to lift. The TREND doesn't replace the TAF -- it's a shorter-range, more specific indicator for the immediate future. Always check both.
exam_tip: TREND = 2-hour forecast appended to METAR/SPECI. NOSIG = no significant change. Uses BECMG and TEMPO like TAFs. Shorter range than TAF, more immediate indicator.
key_facts: TREND: short-period forecast covering next 2 hours from observation time
Appended to METAR or SPECI
Uses BECMG (permanent change) and TEMPO (temporary fluctuations)
NOSIG = no significant change expected in next 2 hours
Covers: wind, visibility, weather, cloud changes
Quick-look tool for immediate future -- use alongside TAF, not instead of
source: AC 61-5 Rev 38, AIPNZ MET section, ICAO Annex 3

syllabus_desc: Describe the global distribution of surface pressure systems and their seasonal migration.
concept: The earth's surface pressure pattern follows the general circulation. At the equator: the ITCZ, a belt of low pressure. Around 30 degrees latitude: the subtropical high pressure belt (where the descending branch of the Hadley cell creates persistent highs). Around 60 degrees: the sub-polar low pressure trough (where the Ferrel and Polar cells meet at the polar front). At the poles: polar highs (cold, dense air at the surface). These belts migrate north and south with the seasons, following the sun. In the Southern Hemisphere summer, the whole pattern shifts south, bringing the subtropical high closer to NZ and often producing settled summer weather. In winter, the pattern shifts north, the westerly belt contracts, and NZ is more exposed to vigorous frontal activity from the roaring forties. The semi-permanent features of the South Pacific are: the South Pacific Subtropical High (often split into separate cells), the ITCZ to the north, and the circumpolar trough to the south. Understanding these semi-permanent features and their seasonal shifts helps you interpret the prognosis charts and anticipate the likely weather pattern.
exam_tip: Pressure belts: equatorial low, subtropical highs (~30 degrees), sub-polar lows (~60 degrees), polar highs. They migrate seasonally -- south in summer, north in winter.
key_facts: Equatorial trough (ITCZ): low pressure, convergence, convective weather
Subtropical high pressure belt: ~30 degrees, fine weather, descending air
Sub-polar low pressure trough: ~60 degrees, polar front, active weather
Polar highs: cold dense surface air
Belts migrate south in SH summer, north in SH winter
NZ: subtropical high closer in summer (settled), more exposed to westerlies in winter
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Explain the transition altitude and transition level in New Zealand, and describe the procedures for changing altimeter settings.
concept: In NZ, the transition altitude is 11,000 ft AMSL. Below this altitude, you fly on QNH (local altimeter setting referenced to mean sea level). Above the transition altitude, you set the standard pressure of 1013.25 hPa and fly flight levels. The transition level is the lowest flight level available above the transition altitude. The transition layer is the airspace between the two. When climbing through the transition altitude, you change from QNH to standard (1013). When descending through the transition level, you change from standard back to QNH. The reason for flight levels above the transition altitude is vertical separation: if all aircraft use the same reference (1013), the vertical spacing between them is maintained regardless of local pressure variations. With QNH, two aircraft using different regional QNH values could appear to be at different altitudes when they're actually at the same flight level. For CPL IFR operations, getting the transition procedure right is fundamental. ATC will assign you a flight level for the cruise and give you the QNH for descent. Set the QNH when instructed (or when descending through the transition level if not instructed earlier) and confirm the change.
exam_tip: NZ transition altitude: 11,000 ft. Below: QNH (altitude AMSL). Above: standard 1013.25 hPa (flight levels). Climbing: set 1013 passing transition altitude. Descending: set QNH when instructed.
key_facts: NZ transition altitude: 11,000 ft AMSL
Below transition altitude: QNH (altitude AMSL)
Above transition altitude: standard 1013.25 hPa (flight levels)
Transition layer: airspace between transition altitude and transition level
Flight levels ensure vertical separation using common reference
Climbing: set 1013 at transition altitude. Descending: set QNH when instructed
source: AIPNZ ENR section, NZ CAR Part 91, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the relationship between wind and the movement of pressure systems for flight planning.
concept: Understanding how wind relates to pressure systems lets you use wind observations to verify or challenge the forecast. The key principles for the Southern Hemisphere: wind circulates clockwise around lows and anticlockwise around highs. Near the surface, friction causes the wind to cross isobars toward low pressure (at about 30 degrees over land). If you know the wind direction, Buys Ballot's Law tells you where the low is. For flight planning, this has practical applications: if the surface wind at your departure is from the northwest, the low is likely to the southwest, and conditions will deteriorate as it approaches. If the wind veers from NW to SW during your flight, a cold front has likely passed. Upper-level winds (above the friction layer) flow nearly parallel to isobars -- these are the winds shown on your AAW and wind/temp charts. The difference between surface and upper winds tells you about the friction layer depth and stability. When planning fuel, always use the forecast upper winds at your planned altitude -- surface winds are irrelevant at cruise level.
exam_tip: SH: clockwise around lows, anticlockwise around highs. Surface wind crosses isobars toward low pressure (~30 degrees over land). Upper winds parallel to isobars. Use upper winds for cruise fuel planning.
key_facts: SH: clockwise circulation around lows, anticlockwise around highs
Surface wind crosses isobars toward low pressure at ~30 degrees
Upper winds (above friction layer): nearly parallel to isobars
NW surface wind: low likely to the SW, conditions may deteriorate
Wind veering NW to SW: cold front passage
Use forecast upper winds for fuel planning, not surface winds
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Explain how the Fohn effect produces dramatically different weather on each side of NZ mountain ranges.
concept: The Fohn effect is the single most dramatic weather-maker in NZ because the mountain spine runs roughly north-south, perpendicular to the prevailing westerly flow. In a northwest flow: air approaches the Southern Alps laden with moisture from the Tasman. Forced to rise, it cools at the DALR (3 degrees C per 1000 ft) then the SALR (~1.5 degrees C per 1000 ft) after condensation. Enormous quantities of rain fall on the West Coast -- Milford Sound averages over 6,000 mm annually. The now much drier air descends the eastern side at the DALR, arriving in Canterbury as a hot, dry, gusty nor'wester. If the air starts at 18 degrees C at sea level on the west, rises to 3,000 m dropping to about 3 degrees C, then descends to sea level on the east, it arrives at about 30 degrees C -- a 12-degree temperature gain. This temperature asymmetry also works in reverse: in a southeasterly flow, the east coast gets rain while the west side dries out, though this is less common and less dramatic. For IFR pilots, the Fohn effect means you can depart from one side of the ranges in fine conditions and arrive at the other in heavy rain or, in reverse, in hot gusty turbulence.
exam_tip: NW Fohn: West Coast rain (6,000+ mm/year at Milford), Canterbury hot dry nor'wester. Temperature gain 10-15 degrees C over the Alps. Conditions on either side of the ranges can be dramatically different.
key_facts: NZ mountain spine perpendicular to prevailing westerlies maximises Fohn effect
NW flow: West Coast heavy rain (Milford 6,000+ mm/year), Canterbury hot dry
Temperature gain of 10-15 degrees C crossing the Alps is possible
Reverse Fohn (SE flow): east coast rain, west coast dries out (less common)
IFR significance: radically different conditions either side of the ranges
Fohn wind produces severe turbulence and mountain wave in the lee
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the cloud types associated with each lifting mechanism and their significance for flight planning.
concept: Each lifting mechanism produces characteristic cloud types, and knowing the link helps you read the sky and plan your flight. Orographic lift in stable air: stratus, stratocumulus, or altostratus -- layered cloud hugging the terrain. In unstable air: cumulus and cumulonimbus develop on the windward slopes. Convective lift: cumulus humilis (fair weather), cumulus mediocris, cumulus congestus (towering cumulus), and in the extreme, cumulonimbus -- all heaped, well-defined cloud types. Frontal lift: warm fronts produce a classic sequence of cirrus -> cirrostratus -> altostratus -> nimbostratus as the front approaches. Cold fronts produce cumulus, cumulonimbus, and stratocumulus in the post-frontal air. Convergence: can produce any cloud type depending on stability, but often supports cumuliform development. For flight planning, the cloud type tells you about turbulence (cumuliform = bumpy, stratiform = smooth), icing risk (deep cumuliform = worst icing), precipitation type (continuous rain = stratiform, showers = cumuliform), and how long conditions will last (frontal stratiform can persist for hours, convective showers are shorter-lived).
exam_tip: Orographic + stable = stratus. Convective = cumulus types. Warm front sequence: Ci -> Cs -> As -> Ns. Cloud type predicts: turbulence type, icing risk, precipitation character, and duration.
key_facts: Orographic + stable air = stratus/stratocumulus (layered on terrain)
Orographic + unstable air = cumulus/cumulonimbus on windward slopes
Convective lift = cumulus series (humilis -> mediocris -> congestus -> Cb)
Warm front: Ci -> Cs -> As -> Ns (progressive lowering and thickening)
Cold front: Cb along front, Sc and Cu in post-frontal air
Cloud type predicts turbulence, icing, precipitation type, and duration
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the hazards of snow, sleet, and hail to light aircraft operations.
concept: Snow, sleet, and hail each present distinct hazards to light aircraft. Snow: accumulates on the airframe (increasing weight and drag), restricts engine air intakes, reduces visibility dramatically when heavy, and can obscure runway markings. Wet snow is heavier and sticks more readily than dry snow. Snow on the ground creates a white-out effect that eliminates horizon references and makes depth perception difficult during landing. Sleet: partially melted snow, indicating the freezing level is close to the surface. Sleet transitions to rain below and snow above, so encountering sleet tells you the temperature is hovering around zero -- prime conditions for airframe icing. Sleet also reduces visibility and creates slippery runway surfaces. Hail: the most immediately dangerous. Even small hail at flight speeds can crack windscreens, dent leading edges, and damage engine intakes. Large hail can cause catastrophic damage. Hail is associated with mature cumulonimbus -- its presence confirms severe convective activity. Never penetrate an area of known hail. In NZ, hail is most common with vigorous cold fronts and summer thunderstorms. Snow to low levels occurs during strong southerly outbreaks in winter.
exam_tip: Snow: weight, drag, reduced visibility, white-out. Sleet: indicates freezing level near surface, prime icing conditions. Hail: structural damage, associated with mature Cb -- never penetrate.
key_facts: Snow: accumulates on airframe, reduces visibility, white-out on ground
Wet snow heavier and stickier than dry snow
Sleet: partially melted snow, freezing level near surface, icing risk
Hail: can crack windscreens and damage airframe even at small sizes
Large hail: catastrophic damage possible -- associated with mature Cb
NZ hail: cold fronts and summer thunderstorms. Snow: winter southerly outbreaks
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the South Pacific Convergence Zone (SPCZ) and its influence on weather affecting New Zealand.
concept: The South Pacific Convergence Zone (SPCZ) is a band of convergence and associated cloud and rainfall that extends from the western Pacific warm pool southeastward across the South Pacific, roughly from Papua New Guinea through Fiji and Tonga toward the Cook Islands. It's one of the most persistent weather features in the Southern Hemisphere. The SPCZ is driven by the convergence of southeast trade winds with monsoon westerlies and produces a semi-permanent band of thunderstorms, heavy rainfall, and tropical disturbances. While the SPCZ doesn't directly sit over NZ, its position and intensity influence what tropical moisture and weather systems track southward toward NZ. When the SPCZ is active and extends further south (especially during La Nina events), NZ receives more tropical moisture, more ex-tropical cyclones, and generally wetter conditions, particularly in the upper North Island. During El Nino, the SPCZ shifts northeast, NZ tends to get more westerly flow, and the North Island can be drier than normal. Understanding the SPCZ position helps explain why some summers are tropical and wet while others are dry and dominated by westerlies.
exam_tip: SPCZ: band of convergence extending SE from western Pacific. Active SPCZ closer to NZ = more tropical moisture and weather. La Nina enhances SPCZ toward NZ; El Nino shifts it northeast.
key_facts: SPCZ: semi-permanent band of convergence across the South Pacific
Extends from western Pacific warm pool SE through Fiji/Tonga region
Produces thunderstorms, heavy rainfall, and tropical disturbances
Active SPCZ closer to NZ: more tropical moisture, wetter conditions
La Nina: SPCZ extends further south toward NZ
El Nino: SPCZ shifts northeast, NZ gets more westerly flow
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ, NIWA

syllabus_desc: Describe the selection of an alternate aerodrome based on meteorological considerations.
concept: Alternate selection is one of the most important meteorological decisions in IFR flight planning. The fundamental rule: your alternate must have weather conditions that are genuinely different and better than your destination. This means selecting an alternate that's meteorologically independent -- not subject to the same weather system. If your destination is fogged in due to radiation fog (a local phenomenon), an alternate 50 km away on the same plain may also be fogged in. Choose one in the hills, on the coast, or on the other side of the ranges. If your destination is below minima due to a warm front, choose an alternate that the front has already passed or hasn't yet reached. Consider: the aerodrome must have an approach type you're qualified for, the forecast conditions at your ETA must be above the required minima for that approach, and you must have sufficient fuel to fly to your destination, conduct an approach, fly a missed approach, then fly to the alternate and conduct an approach there, plus final reserve. In NZ, classic alternate strategies include: Auckland alternate for Hamilton (different terrain, often different fog pattern), Nelson or Blenheim alternate for Wellington (lee side of ranges vs funnelled strait), Timaru or Oamaru as alternates for Christchurch fog.
exam_tip: Alternate must be meteorologically independent of destination. If fog at destination: choose different terrain/geography. If frontal weather: choose ahead of or behind the front. Must have fuel for approach, missed approach, diversion, and reserve.
key_facts: Alternate must be meteorologically independent of destination
Different weather system, different terrain, different fog pattern
Forecast conditions at ETA must be above approach minima for that aerodrome
Fuel required: destination approach + missed approach + alternate + final reserve
NZ strategies: Auckland alt for Hamilton, Nelson/Blenheim alt for Wellington
Consider approach types available at the alternate
source: NZ CAR Part 91, AIPNZ ENR section, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Explain how to assess the reliability and currency of meteorological information for flight planning decisions.
concept: Not all meteorological information has the same shelf life, and understanding the reliability of each product helps you make better decisions. METARs are snapshots -- they're accurate at the time of observation but conditions can change quickly. A METAR that's 30 minutes old at a stable, high-pressure aerodrome is still reliable; the same age at an aerodrome with an approaching front may be significantly outdated. TAFs are forecasts with defined accuracy standards, but they're less reliable at the ends of their validity period and in rapidly changing conditions. GRAFORs and SIGWX charts are area forecasts -- they capture the big picture well but may not reflect local variations. PIREPs are real-time but anecdotal -- one pilot's 'moderate turbulence' might be another's 'severe', and the report only applies to the specific location and time. The general principle: the more recent the information, the more reliable. Always check the issue time and validity period. If your weather information is ageing and conditions are changing, update it. ATC and Flight Information Service can provide updated METARs and TAFs in flight. Don't fly into uncertainty based on stale information when current data is available.
exam_tip: METAR reliability drops quickly in changing conditions. TAFs less reliable at the edge of validity. PIREPs are real-time but subjective. Always check issue time and validity. Update stale information before making decisions.
key_facts: METARs: snapshots, reliability depends on how fast conditions are changing
TAFs: less reliable at ends of validity period and in rapidly changing conditions
GRAFORs/SIGWX: good big picture, may miss local variations
PIREPs: real-time but subjective and location-specific
Always check issue time and validity period of all products
Update stale information from ATC or Flight Information Service in flight
source: AIPNZ MET section, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the life cycle of a mid-latitude depression and how it affects NZ weather.
concept: Mid-latitude depressions (lows) follow a predictable life cycle that directly determines the weather sequence across NZ. The cycle begins when a wave develops on the polar front -- a small disturbance where warm air pushes poleward and cold air pushes equatorward. This is the wave stage -- a shallow low forms at the tip of the wave with a warm front extending ahead and a cold front trailing behind. As the low deepens (the pressure falls further), the fronts become more defined and the weather more intense -- this is the developing stage. The mature stage is when the low reaches its deepest central pressure -- the strongest winds, heaviest rain, and most vigorous frontal activity. The cold front moves faster than the warm front and eventually catches it, beginning the occlusion process. In the occluded stage, the warm air is progressively lifted off the surface as the cold front undercuts the warm sector. The low fills (pressure rises) and the system weakens. Depressions affecting NZ typically develop over the Tasman Sea, mature as they approach or cross the country, and occlude as they move off to the east. The whole cycle typically takes 3-7 days.
exam_tip: Depression lifecycle: wave -> developing -> mature (deepest, strongest) -> occluded -> filling. NZ lows typically develop over the Tasman and occlude moving eastward. Full cycle: 3-7 days.
key_facts: Wave stage: disturbance on polar front, shallow low forms
Developing stage: low deepens, fronts become more defined
Mature stage: deepest pressure, strongest winds and weather
Occluded stage: cold front catches warm front, warm air lifted
Filling stage: low pressure rises, system weakens
NZ lows: develop over Tasman, mature crossing NZ, occlude moving east
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Define air masses and describe the two main air masses affecting New Zealand.
concept: An air mass is a large body of air (hundreds of kilometres across) with relatively uniform temperature and moisture characteristics acquired from the surface over which it formed. Two main air masses routinely affect NZ. Tropical maritime (mT) air originates over the warm subtropical ocean to the north and northeast. It's warm, moist, and unstable -- bringing cloud, rain, poor visibility, and a high freezing level. When mT air flows over NZ from the north, expect widespread low cloud, drizzle, and muggy conditions. Polar maritime (mP) air originates over the cold Southern Ocean to the south and southwest. It's cold, relatively moist (having crossed ocean), and initially stable near the surface but becoming unstable as it moves over the warmer NZ seas -- producing convective showers, good visibility between showers, and a low freezing level. Post-frontal weather in NZ is typically mP air: cold, showery, clearing. The contrast between these two air masses at the polar front is what creates NZ's frontal systems. Modified polar air (that has spent time over warmer water) and modified tropical air (cooled by more southern SSTs) represent intermediate conditions.
exam_tip: Tropical maritime (mT): warm, moist, low cloud, drizzle, high freezing level. Polar maritime (mP): cold, showery, good visibility between, low freezing level. Their interaction at the polar front creates NZ fronts.
key_facts: Air mass: large body of air with uniform temperature and moisture
Tropical maritime (mT): warm, moist, from subtropical north, unstable
mT brings: low cloud, drizzle, poor visibility, high freezing level
Polar maritime (mP): cold, from Southern Ocean, showery, low freezing level
mP brings: convective showers, good vis between, cold temperatures
Contrast between mT and mP at polar front creates NZ's frontal systems
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain how stability affects cloud type, turbulence, and visibility.
concept: The stability of the atmosphere determines the character of the weather you'll encounter. In stable air: vertical motion is suppressed, so you get layered (stratiform) cloud, smooth flight conditions (little turbulence), but poor visibility below cloud due to trapped moisture, haze, and smoke. Precipitation is steady and continuous (drizzle, steady rain). Winds tend to be steady with little gustiness. In unstable air: vertical motion is enhanced, producing heaped (cumuliform) cloud, bumpy conditions (convective turbulence), but generally good visibility outside showers because the vertical mixing disperses haze and moisture. Precipitation is showery -- heavy but short-lived. Winds are gusty because turbulent eddies bring faster air from above to the surface. For IFR planning, the stability determines your experience: a stable warm sector means instrument conditions with low stratus, drizzle, and marginal visibility for hours. An unstable post-frontal air mass means showers and turbulence but often good conditions between cells. You can often read the stability from the cloud type before checking the detailed weather -- stratiform cloud warns of stable, persistent IFR conditions.
exam_tip: Stable air: stratiform cloud, smooth air, poor visibility, continuous precipitation. Unstable air: cumuliform cloud, bumpy, good vis between showers, gusty winds. Cloud type is your quick stability indicator.
key_facts: Stable air: stratiform cloud, smooth, poor visibility, continuous drizzle/rain
Unstable air: cumuliform cloud, turbulent, good vis between showers, gusty
Stable conditions can persist for hours -- prolonged IFR
Unstable conditions: showers are heavy but short, gaps between cells
Cloud type is your quick visual stability indicator
Stability determines the character of IFR conditions you'll encounter
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the gradient wind and the difference between geostrophic and gradient wind around curved isobars.
concept: The geostrophic wind is the theoretical balance between the pressure gradient force and Coriolis force -- it flows parallel to straight isobars. But isobars around real weather systems are curved, and when air follows a curved path, centripetal acceleration enters the equation. The gradient wind accounts for this curvature. Around a low pressure system (cyclonic flow in the SH -- clockwise), the centripetal acceleration acts in the same direction as the pressure gradient force, so Coriolis doesn't need to be as strong to maintain balance -- the wind speed is less than geostrophic (subgeostrophic). Around a high (anticyclonic flow -- anticlockwise), centripetal acceleration opposes the pressure gradient force, so Coriolis needs to be stronger, which means the wind speed is greater than geostrophic (supergeostrophic). The tighter the curvature, the bigger the difference. Practically, this means: for the same isobar spacing, wind around a tight low is weaker than you'd estimate from the geostrophic approximation, and wind around a tight high is stronger. This is a CPL-level distinction that explains occasional surprises when the actual wind doesn't match a simple isobar-spacing estimate.
exam_tip: Gradient wind around a low: subgeostrophic (less than geostrophic). Around a high: supergeostrophic (more than geostrophic). Tighter curvature = bigger difference from geostrophic.
key_facts: Geostrophic wind: PGF balanced by Coriolis, flows parallel to straight isobars
Gradient wind: accounts for curvature of isobars (centripetal acceleration)
Around a low (cyclonic): subgeostrophic -- wind weaker than geostrophic estimate
Around a high (anticyclonic): supergeostrophic -- wind stronger than geostrophic
Tighter curvature = bigger difference between gradient and geostrophic wind
Explains why wind around tight highs can be surprisingly strong
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Explain Runway Visual Range (RVR) and its application to IFR instrument approaches.
concept: Runway Visual Range (RVR) is the distance over which the pilot of an aircraft on the runway centreline can see the runway surface markings or lights. It's measured by instruments (transmissometers) positioned along the runway and reported when the prevailing visibility drops below 1,500 m. RVR is more operationally relevant than prevailing visibility for instrument approaches because it measures what you'll actually see during the final phase of approach and landing. RVR is reported in the METAR as an R group, for example R06/0400 means RVR for runway 06 is 400 metres. Multiple sensors along the runway may give touchdown, mid-point, and stop-end RVR values. Trend indicators may be appended: U (upward/improving), D (downward/deteriorating), N (no change). For IFR operations, each approach type has a minimum RVR or visibility requirement. Category I ILS typically requires 550 m RVR or 800 m visibility. If the RVR drops below your approach minimum, you cannot continue below the decision altitude. In NZ, RVR is reported at the major IFR aerodromes (Auckland, Wellington, Christchurch) and is available on the ATIS and in METARs.
exam_tip: RVR: measured visibility along the runway, reported when visibility < 1,500 m. More relevant than prevailing visibility for approaches. Cat I ILS typically requires 550 m RVR. Trend: U (up), D (down), N (no change).
key_facts: RVR: distance pilot can see runway markings/lights from the centreline
Reported when prevailing visibility drops below 1,500 m
METAR format: R06/0400 means RVR for runway 06 is 400 metres
Trend indicators: U (improving), D (deteriorating), N (no change)
Each approach type has minimum RVR requirement (Cat I ILS: typically 550 m)
NZ: RVR reported at Auckland, Wellington, Christchurch
source: AIPNZ MET section, ICAO Annex 3, NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the icing intensity reporting scale and its operational significance for CPL flight planning.
concept: Icing intensity is reported on a four-level scale and the terms have specific operational meanings. Trace: ice becomes perceptible, rate of accumulation is barely greater than rate of sublimation. No hazard if encounter is brief. De-icing/anti-icing equipment not needed. Light: rate of accumulation may create a problem if flight continues in the environment for over an hour. Occasional use of de-icing/anti-icing equipment clears the accumulation. Aircraft without icing protection should consider exiting. Moderate: rate of accumulation is such that even short encounters are potentially hazardous. De-icing/anti-icing equipment is necessary and used frequently. Diversion may be necessary. Severe: rate of accumulation is such that de-icing/anti-icing equipment cannot cope. Immediate exit from the environment is essential. Icing forecasts on GRAFORs and SIGWX charts use these same terms. When the GRAFOR shows 'MOD ICE 5000/12000', it means moderate icing is forecast between 5,000 ft and 12,000 ft. If your aircraft has no icing protection and the forecast shows light icing or worse at your planned altitude in cloud, you need to plan to avoid that altitude in cloud or choose a different route.
exam_tip: Trace: barely perceptible. Light: problem after 1+ hour, de-ice clears it. Moderate: short encounters hazardous, de-ice needed often. Severe: de-ice can't cope, exit immediately.
key_facts: Trace: barely perceptible, no hazard if brief
Light: problem after 1+ hour exposure, de-icing clears accumulation
Moderate: short encounters potentially hazardous, de-icing needed frequently
Severe: de-icing cannot cope, immediate exit essential
GRAFOR format: MOD ICE 5000/12000 = moderate icing 5,000-12,000 ft
No icing protection + light or worse forecast = avoid that altitude in cloud
source: ICAO Annex 3, AIPNZ MET section, AFT ATPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the influence of El Nino and La Nina (ENSO) on New Zealand weather patterns.
concept: The El Nino-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) cycle is the most significant large-scale influence on NZ's interannual weather variability. El Nino occurs when sea surface temperatures in the central and eastern tropical Pacific become warmer than normal. For NZ, this typically means: stronger and more frequent westerly and southwesterly winds (especially in summer), more rainfall on the West Coast and the southern South Island, and drier conditions in the northeast North Island. The westerly flow brings more mountain wave activity and frontal weather. La Nina is the opposite phase -- cooler than normal SSTs in the central Pacific. For NZ, La Nina brings: more northeasterly winds, warmer and more humid conditions, more rainfall in the northeast North Island (Northland, Auckland, Bay of Plenty), and higher likelihood of ex-tropical cyclones reaching NZ from the north. The SPCZ tends to be more active and further south during La Nina. Neutral ENSO conditions fall between the two. MetService and NIWA provide seasonal forecasts that incorporate ENSO predictions, and at CPL level you should be aware of the current ENSO state when interpreting seasonal weather patterns and understanding why certain weather features are more or less common in a given year.
exam_tip: El Nino: more westerly flow, West Coast wetter, NE North Island drier. La Nina: more NE flow, upper North Island wetter, more tropical cyclone risk. Check NIWA and MetService seasonal outlooks.
key_facts: El Nino: warmer central/eastern tropical Pacific SSTs
El Nino NZ effect: more westerly/SW wind, West Coast wetter, NE NI drier
La Nina: cooler central tropical Pacific SSTs
La Nina NZ effect: more NE wind, upper North Island wetter, more tropical cyclone risk
La Nina: SPCZ more active and further south
MetService and NIWA provide seasonal forecasts incorporating ENSO predictions
source: NIWA, MetService NZ, AFT ATPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Explain how to use wind and temperature forecasts for navigation log calculations and performance planning.
concept: Wind and temperature forecasts feed directly into your navigation log and performance calculations. For nav log work: the AAW gives you forecast wind direction and speed at various flight levels for each NZ aviation area. You use this to calculate your heading (to maintain track allowing for wind), groundspeed (affected by headwind/tailwind component), and therefore your ETAs and fuel burn for each leg. The wind/temp charts provide similar information in a graphical format, useful for identifying the best cruising level (choose the altitude with the most favourable wind component to minimise fuel burn). For performance planning: forecast temperature at your planned altitude determines the ISA deviation, which affects true airspeed, rate of climb, and engine performance. A temperature significantly above ISA means reduced climb performance and longer takeoff distances. At your destination, surface temperature and pressure determine the density altitude for landing performance. Remember: AAW winds are in degrees TRUE -- you'll need to convert to magnetic for heading calculations using the local magnetic variation. And always carry fuel reserves beyond your calculated requirement, because the forecast winds may not match actual conditions.
exam_tip: AAW winds are in degrees TRUE -- convert to magnetic for heading calculations. Choose cruising level with best wind component to minimise fuel. Forecast temperature determines ISA deviation for performance.
key_facts: AAW: forecast wind at flight levels for each NZ aviation area
