Used to calculate heading, groundspeed, ETA, and fuel burn
Wind/temp charts: choose cruising level with most favourable wind
Forecast temperature: determines ISA deviation for performance calculations
AAW winds in degrees TRUE -- convert to magnetic for heading
Always carry fuel reserves beyond calculated requirement
source: AFT CPL Meteorology, AIPNZ MET section

syllabus_desc: Describe the typical weather effects of a strong southerly outbreak on New Zealand.
concept: A strong southerly outbreak occurs when a deep low passes to the east of NZ and cold polar or sub-Antarctic air surges northward up the country behind the associated cold front. The effects can be dramatic. The temperature drops sharply -- sometimes 10-15 degrees C in a few hours. Snow falls to low levels, sometimes to sea level in the South Island and to 200-400 m in the North Island during severe events. The wind is strong and gusty from the south or southwest. The southern and eastern coasts of both islands are most exposed, with coastal surf and storm surge added to the wind and precipitation hazards. Visibility is generally good between snow or hail showers but can drop to near zero in heavy snow. The freezing level may drop to near sea level, creating icing risk at all altitudes in cloud. Mountain wave activity is common as the strong flow crosses the ranges. For aviation, a severe southerly outbreak is a significant event: aerodromes can close for snow, the freezing level may be at or below circuit altitude, turbulence is severe near terrain, and conditions can change faster than forecasts update. The good news is that southerly outbreaks are usually well-forecast 24-48 hours ahead.
exam_tip: Strong southerly: sharp temp drop (10-15 degrees C), snow to low levels, gusty SW winds, near-zero freezing level, mountain waves. Usually well-forecast 24-48 hours ahead.
key_facts: Deep low passes east of NZ, cold polar air surges northward
Temperature drops 10-15 degrees C in hours
Snow to sea level in South Island, 200-400 m in North Island (severe events)
Strong gusty south-southwest winds
Freezing level near sea level -- icing at all altitudes in cloud
Mountain wave activity, severe turbulence near terrain, aerodromes may close
source: MetService NZ, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the typical weather associated with a blocking high pressure system over or near New Zealand.
concept: A blocking high is a large, slow-moving or stationary anticyclone that disrupts the normal west-to-east progression of weather systems. When a blocking high parks over or near NZ, the effects depend on the season and your position relative to it. Under the high itself: fine weather, light winds, clear skies by day, but radiation fog in valleys and sheltered areas overnight (especially in autumn and winter). The subsidence inversion traps haze and moisture below it, so visibility can be poor at low levels even though the sky looks blue above. Blocking highs can last for days or even weeks. While the weather under the high is fine, the systems that would normally pass through NZ are forced to track around it -- this can divert fronts to the north or south, creating unusual weather patterns. If a low is blocked from passing through, it can stall and intensify, producing prolonged heavy rainfall on the side of NZ that faces it. In summer, extended blocking highs bring drought conditions to the areas under the high. In winter, they bring clear nights and hard frosts. For aviation, the main hazards under a blocking high are fog, reduced low-level visibility from trapped haze, and the potential for unusual weather around the edges of the block.
exam_tip: Blocking high: slow-moving, disrupts normal west-to-east flow. Under it: fine but fog risk, subsidence inversion traps haze. Systems forced around it -- unusual weather patterns on the edges. Can last days to weeks.
key_facts: Blocking high: large, slow-moving anticyclone disrupting normal flow
Under the high: fine weather, light winds, but radiation fog overnight
Subsidence inversion traps haze below -- poor low-level visibility possible
Weather systems forced around the block, creating unusual patterns
Stalled lows can intensify, producing prolonged rainfall on exposed side
Can last days to weeks -- summer drought, winter frosts, persistent fog
source: MetService NZ, AFT ATPL Meteorology

topic: 20.2 -- Regional Meteorological Services, Reports and Forecasts
syllabus_desc: Describe the meteorological services provided by MetService NZ for aviation, including gopreflight.
concept: MetService NZ is the designated meteorological authority for aviation weather in New Zealand, fulfilling NZ's obligations under ICAO Annex 3. Their primary aviation platform is gopreflight (gopreflight.metservice.com), which provides the full suite of aviation weather products. At CPL level, you should be able to navigate gopreflight efficiently to access: METARs and SPECIs for all reporting aerodromes, TAFs for aerodromes that have them, GRAFORs for area weather, the SIGMET page for current hazard warnings, SIGWX charts and wind/temp charts for planning, satellite and radar imagery for real-time weather picture, and AAW for upper winds. MetService also provides aviation weather briefings by phone for pilots who need to discuss conditions with a forecaster -- don't hesitate to use this service when conditions are complex or you want a second opinion on the weather. The AIPNZ MET section describes all available products, their formats, and their update schedules. A good habit is to check the latest products just before departure and note the next expected update time, so you know when to check again if your flight is delayed.
exam_tip: MetService NZ is the designated aviation met authority. gopreflight is the primary platform for all aviation weather products. Phone briefings are available for complex weather situations.
key_facts: MetService NZ: designated aviation meteorological authority under ICAO Annex 3
gopreflight.metservice.com: primary aviation weather platform
Products: METARs, SPECIs, TAFs, GRAFORs, SIGMETs, SIGWX, wind/temp, satellite, radar
Phone briefings available for complex weather discussions
AIPNZ MET section describes all products, formats, and update schedules
Check latest products just before departure, note next update time
source: AIPNZ MET section, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the greenhouse effect and its role in heating the lower atmosphere.
concept: The greenhouse effect is the process by which certain gases in the atmosphere absorb and re-radiate terrestrial (long-wave) radiation, trapping heat near the earth's surface. Without it, the earth's average surface temperature would be about -18 degrees C instead of the actual +15 degrees C -- making the planet uninhabitable. The main greenhouse gases are water vapour (by far the most significant for day-to-day weather), carbon dioxide, methane, and ozone. The process works like this: insolation (short-wave radiation from the sun) passes through the atmosphere relatively freely and heats the earth's surface. The surface re-radiates this energy as long-wave (infrared) radiation. Greenhouse gases absorb this outgoing radiation and re-radiate it in all directions -- including back toward the surface. This trapping effect keeps the lower atmosphere warmer than it would otherwise be. For aviation meteorology, the greenhouse effect explains why the atmosphere is heated from below (the surface absorbs insolation, then terrestrial radiation warms the air through absorption by water vapour), why temperature normally decreases with altitude, and why water vapour content is so important for weather -- it's both the key weather-producing gas and the primary greenhouse gas.
exam_tip: Greenhouse effect: water vapour and CO2 absorb terrestrial (long-wave) radiation and re-radiate it, warming the lower atmosphere. Without it, earth would be -18 degrees C. Explains why atmosphere is heated from below.
key_facts: Greenhouse gases absorb outgoing terrestrial (long-wave) radiation
Re-radiate heat in all directions, including back toward surface
Water vapour is the most significant greenhouse gas for daily weather
Without greenhouse effect: earth's average temp would be ~-18 degrees C
Explains why atmosphere is heated from below (surface absorbs, re-radiates)
Why temperature normally decreases with altitude in the troposphere
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the operational characteristics of cloud sensors (ceilometers) in automated weather stations.
concept: Automated Weather Stations (AWS) at NZ aerodromes use ceilometers to measure cloud base height. A ceilometer fires a laser beam vertically and measures the time for the backscattered light to return from cloud. At CPL level, you need to understand the limitations of this technology because they directly affect the reliability of METAR AUTO cloud reports. The ceilometer measures cloud in a very narrow cone directly overhead -- it doesn't see cloud approaching from the sides. This means it can miss cloud that's about to move over the aerodrome, and it can underreport cloud amount because its sampling area is so small. If cloud is scattered and moving, the ceilometer may report FEW when the actual amount is SCT or BKN. The sensor typically integrates readings over a 30-minute window to build a cloud amount picture, which smooths out some variability but can also lag behind rapidly changing conditions. Additionally, the ceilometer can't always distinguish cloud type -- it reports what it measures overhead, regardless of whether it's stratus, stratocumulus, or the base of a cumulonimbus. When conditions are marginal, always cross-reference METAR AUTO cloud reports with the TAF and any available human observations or pilot reports for a more complete picture.
exam_tip: Ceilometer: narrow vertical beam, can underreport cloud amount. Can't see cloud approaching from sides. Integrates over ~30 minutes. Can lag rapidly changing conditions. Cross-reference with TAF and PIREPs.
key_facts: Ceilometer: laser beam fired vertically, measures backscatter from cloud
Very narrow cone overhead -- can miss approaching cloud
Can underreport cloud amount due to small sampling area
Integrates readings over ~30 minutes to build cloud amount picture
Can lag behind rapidly changing conditions
Cross-reference METAR AUTO with TAF and PIREPs when conditions are marginal
source: AIPNZ MET section, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the electrical hazards of thunderstorms and the effects of lightning strikes on aircraft.
concept: Thunderstorms are powerful electrical generators. The charge separation mechanism works through collisions between ice crystals and graupel (soft hail) in the updraft -- positive charges accumulate near the cloud top, negative charges in the lower part. Lightning is the discharge of this built-up potential -- it can occur within the cloud (most common), between clouds, from cloud to ground, or from cloud to clear air. Aircraft can trigger lightning strikes by flying through areas of strong electric field -- the aircraft acts as a conductive bridge between areas of different charge. A lightning strike on an aircraft typically enters and exits through extremities (wing tips, tail, nose cone). Effects include: temporary blinding of the crew (especially at night), damage to avionics and electronic instruments, magnetisation of the compass (requiring a compass swing afterward), burns or holes in the skin (usually minor in metal aircraft, more concerning in composite), and in rare cases, fuel ignition risk. Modern metal aircraft act as a Faraday cage, protecting occupants, but composite aircraft need specific lightning protection design. The safety advice is simple: avoid thunderstorms. If you see lightning, the storm is too close for comfort.
exam_tip: Lightning enters/exits aircraft extremities (wing tips, tail, nose). Effects: crew blinding, avionics damage, compass magnetisation, skin burns. Metal aircraft = Faraday cage. Composite aircraft need specific lightning protection.
key_facts: Charge separation: ice crystal and graupel collisions in updraft
Lightning types: within cloud (most common), cloud-to-cloud, cloud-to-ground
Aircraft can trigger lightning in strong electric fields
Enters/exits aircraft extremities (wing tips, tail, nose cone)
Effects: temporary blinding, avionics damage, compass magnetisation, skin damage
Metal aircraft = Faraday cage protection; composite aircraft need specific design
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe low-level jet streams and their effect on low-level wind shear in New Zealand.
concept: Low-level jet streams (LLJs) are narrow bands of strong wind in the lowest few thousand feet of the atmosphere, typically occurring at night when the surface-based inversion decouples the air above from surface friction. Without friction, the air above the inversion accelerates -- sometimes to 30-40 kt or more at just 500-1,500 ft AGL while the surface wind is nearly calm. In NZ, LLJs are common on clear nights with strong pressure gradients, especially in the Canterbury Plains and other flat areas east of the ranges. The aviation significance is wind shear: as you climb through the inversion layer on a night departure, you can encounter a sudden increase in wind speed that requires immediate adjustment. On approach, descending through the LLJ means losing a significant headwind or gaining a tailwind component, both of which reduce your airspeed and can destabilise the approach. The LLJ typically dissipates after sunrise when convective mixing re-establishes friction coupling with the surface. In nocturnal IFR operations, always check the AAW and be alert for LLJ conditions -- a large difference between surface wind and the forecast 2,000 ft wind is a red flag.
exam_tip: Low-level jet: strong wind at 500-1,500 ft AGL, surface calm, common on clear nights. Causes significant wind shear on departure and approach. Dissipates after sunrise. Large surface-to-2,000 ft wind difference = red flag.
key_facts: Low-level jet: strong wind in lowest 500-1,500 ft, often 30-40 kt
Forms at night when inversion decouples upper air from surface friction
Surface wind may be calm while LLJ is 30-40 kt just above
NZ: common on Canterbury Plains and flat eastern areas on clear nights
Causes wind shear on departure (sudden speed increase) and approach (speed loss)
Dissipates after sunrise when convective mixing resumes
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, MetService NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the filing and use of PIREPs as a professional responsibility of CPL holders.
concept: Filing PIREPs (Pilot Reports) is one of the most valuable contributions you can make to aviation safety, and at CPL level it's a professional responsibility, not just good practice. A PIREP provides real-time information about actual conditions at a specific location and altitude -- something no forecast or remote observation can match. When you encounter conditions that differ from the forecast, or that could affect other pilots' safety, file a PIREP. This includes: turbulence (especially moderate or worse), icing (any intensity), cloud base or tops that differ from forecast, visibility different from the METAR/TAF, wind shear, volcanic ash, or any other significant weather. The PIREP format includes: your position (relative to a waypoint), time, altitude, aircraft type, and the conditions observed using standard intensity descriptors. Pass your PIREP to ATC or FIS by radio. The information is then distributed to other pilots and incorporated into the aviation weather system. In NZ, where observation stations can be sparse (especially over water and mountainous terrain), PIREPs are particularly valuable. Your report might be the information that helps another pilot make a safe decision. A culture of regular PIREP filing makes the whole system safer.
exam_tip: Filing PIREPs is a CPL professional responsibility. Report: turbulence, icing, cloud different from forecast, wind shear, volcanic ash. Include: position, time, altitude, aircraft type, conditions. Pass to ATC or FIS.
key_facts: Filing PIREPs is a professional responsibility at CPL level
Report when conditions differ from forecast or could affect other pilots
Report: turbulence, icing, cloud base/tops, visibility, wind shear, volcanic ash
Format: position, time, altitude, aircraft type, observed conditions
Pass to ATC or FIS by radio for distribution
NZ observation network is sparse -- PIREPs are particularly valuable
source: AIPNZ MET section, NZ CAR Part 91, ICAO Annex 3

syllabus_desc: Describe how surface heating and moisture interact to produce convective cloud and determine cloud type.
concept: On a typical NZ summer day, the interaction between surface heating and moisture content determines whether you get fair-weather cumulus, towering cumulus, or full cumulonimbus. As the surface heats up after sunrise, thermals begin to rise. They cool at the DALR (3 degrees C per 1000 ft). When a thermal reaches its dew point (the condensation level), cloud forms -- this is the base of the cumulus. If the ELR is greater than the DALR below cloud base, the thermals are vigorous and the cumulus grows actively. Above cloud base, the cooling rate slows to the SALR (~1.5 degrees C per 1000 ft) because condensation releases latent heat. If the ELR above cloud base is greater than the SALR, the cloud continues to grow -- potentially into towering cumulus or cumulonimbus. The higher the moisture content (lower cloud base = more moisture available), the more latent heat is released during condensation, the more buoyant the air becomes, and the more vigorously the cloud grows. This is why humid days produce bigger thunderstorms than dry days. The equilibrium level -- where the temperature inside the cloud equals the surrounding environment -- determines the cloud top. Strong inversions (especially the tropopause) cap convective development.
exam_tip: More moisture = lower cloud base = more latent heat released = more vigorous convection. Cloud grows if ELR > SALR above cloud base. Inversions cap convective growth. Humid days produce bigger storms.
key_facts: Thermals rise from heated surface, cool at DALR until dew point = cloud base
Above cloud base: cools at SALR, if ELR > SALR, cloud continues growing
More moisture = lower cloud base = more latent heat = stronger convection
Humid days produce more vigorous convective cloud than dry days
Equilibrium level (temp inside = environment) determines cloud top
Inversions (including tropopause) cap convective development
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology
